Another big difference from Chess is that conflicts are resolved using tactical core cards that will feel to you more like rock-paper-scissors. Unlike in Chess, attacking pieces won't always win. Furthermore you will get free moves and other kinds of special actions through playing Imperial edict cards.

The most destructive force in the game are antimatter missiles. You build missiles in stages on your systems and launch them during your turn. These missiles are the reason why the goal of the game — collecting three different deep-defense codes — is so important. Missile detonations destroy everything in a square and even burn up the square itself so that nothing can enter it. Owning three colors of deep-defense codes makes some of your squares immune to antimatter detonations, upsetting the balance of power and giving you a chance to win.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
To run away from Gargamel and his cat, the smurfs left their village and escaped to the woods.
The players must find them in their six hideouts on the board, bringing them in couples back to the village inside a little caddy, moving it in the paths of the wood.
This with the help (or the tricks) found in the 20 "surprise cards", to be revealed when passing near Gargamel's castle.
The player who manages to finish first his mission, moving with the caddy the assigned couples to the village, will win the game.

Players build a football team one player at a time in Fußball Poker, but they're never assured of getting the player they want since all of the other team owners are bidding at the same time as you.

Each player starts the game with €100 million; someone shuffles the 48 player cards, then places them in a face-down pile. On a turn, the active player draws and reveals four cards, then places a bid face-down on the table; while the bid itself is secret, other players can see how many money cards you included in the bid. Each player in turn places a secret bid, then they reveal these bids. Whoever offers the most money takes the footballer of his choice and places it in an empty position in his team line-up on the game board, then the second-highest bidder does the same, and so on. If a player has no money, he takes the last card on offer. The active player position rotates to the left, then players each bid on and draft another card.

In addition to acquiring high-valued footballers, players are trying to fill their team with a proper line-up of players – midfielders in the midfield, three defenders and a keeper in the back, and so on – as they score bonus points when those sections have the properly positioned players in them. When a player drafts an "ersatz" (substitute) footballer, it can replace any footballer already in play and it counts as being in the proper position; the drawback is that an ersatz player is worth no points on its own.

After twelve rounds of drafting, each player will have a full line-up and one player on the sidelines; this player can be substituted for any other footballer already on the team, even if the substitute is not labeled "ersatz".

Players then tally their player points and any bonus points for completing a section with the proper footballers. The player with the highest score wins, with ties being broken by the money still in hand.

The picture boards and a total of 27 picture tile show images for children. Animals from the front and from the back, penguin, pig, cat, raven and dog, a baby in three different versions and many different children. The tiles are spread face-down, each player gets a board. In your turn you turn up a tile: If you have this exact image also on your board, you place the tile on the board. If not, you put the tile back face down. The first to fill his board wins the game.

A game of memory combined with a kind of lotto. Depending on the number of players each players receives 1, 2 or 3 boards. These boards show one of the characters on a color background and a few items belonging to the character. These items are taken out and placed backside up on the table. In your turn you choose a disc: If it fits your board you can place it. The first to fill his board wins the game.

Trivia game with more than 200 questions about coffee, cultivation, roasting, different types of coffee and more. Players choose for each question how many points they want to make and how big a risk they are willing to take.

Aim of the game: Having the highest number of points after a certain number of question cards have been played (number to be decided by the players at the beginning of a game). Points are earned by correctly answering questions.

Gameplay: The starting player takes a card from the card stack, chooses a letter and reads the corresponding question and its answers. There are two types of questions:

- Questions with one to three correct answers: For each question, there are four answers. One to three of them are correct, at least one is wrong. When all the answers have been read, the players place their chip(s) on the betting boards for the answer(s) that they think is/are correct. Each player (including the one who read the question) can place up to three chips. Players who put their chips on one or more wrong answers don't get any points this round. Players with exactly one correct answer get to advance their pawn one space, 2 correct answers means advancing 2 spaces, 3 correct answers 3 spaces.

- Questions whose answers have to be put in the correct order: For each question, there are three answers. When all the answers have been read, players put their chips in the order that they think is correct (the chips are numbered 1-3 on one side). Players with all three chips in the correct order advance three spaces. Players with one correct answer advance one space.

boardgame: The Sproutz: Race to the Stage
description:
The Sproutz: Race to the Stage is an education board game that teaches children about nutrition, exercise and growth hormone deficiencies.

The game play is simple: draw a card, answer a question and if you are correct, move that many spaces. Players collect tokens as they land on specific spaces that can be used as a tiebreaker.

The goal of the game is to be the first person to make it to the stage while making sure you have more tokens than anyone else who might also make it to the stage on their last turn.

The Deck of Change is a deck of 64-cards with 8 numbers and 8 symbols. It is an open game system designed primarily for fun. It is versatile, multipurpose, and mathematically satisfying to play around with.

These cards might just bring out the most fun you can ever have with your  family, friends, and even strangers. Kids from age 7 to 107 can enjoy the different games for 1 to 16 players. The deck has 64 cards, 8 numbers (0-7), and 8 symbols (yin, energy, force, space, matter, entropy, time, yang).

There are 3 versions of the deck. They are practically the same deck. The main difference is in the visuals. Deck #1: The AiTiA is designed for functionality. Deck #2: The Gauntlet is designed for imagination featuring artwork by Patrick Enrique. And, Deck #3: The Cauldron has bigger cards designed for creativity and brainstorming.

Goal is for players to earn as many segments and units as possible and to have the most money on their money tracking card at the end of game play. Player rolls dice and subtracts one from the other, lands on a given segment and must answer a question from a given math strand if they want to gain credit for that segment. Once a segment is owned, player receives a card showing him/her the fees involved when someone else lands on that segment. All fees are derived from different mathematical questions that are related to the name of the segment owned.
Mathopoly offers students the opportunity to learn mathematical concepts and formulas in a fun, relaxed environment.  Mathopoly the math game incorporates the mathematical diversity required to ensure success for kids in today’s challenging curriculum.

Objective:
Reach target line (= opponent's start position) with as many of your 10 cars as possible.
Each player/team starts with 10 cars (red or blue), moving them by die and turn.
For any obstacle (building, park...) you have to make side steps; you may choose ANY of your cars to be moved, opponent's car(s) may be thrown out. Game ends if one player/team has moved all their cars into target line.

10 colourful pictures and an arrow to determine a letter of the alphabet - with these components from Kaleidos A-Z Bilderquiz offers several rule variants. In one game players try to be the first to name a thing on the pictures with the current letter, in the second game players try to find the majority of words and in the team-variant players name words alternately.

Not Under My Roof! is a fast-paced fighting game in which opponents battle their way through a building. Rather than sitting at a table, the cards you play knock your opponent from room to room until somebody is tossed through an exit. Some cards work better if an object like a lamp or TV is in the room with you. You will fight through the building until somebody throws you out and yells NOT UNDER MY ROOF!
(From The Game Crafter page)

Objective: Avoid collecting too many plastic 'French fries' 
(= penalties you must take if the potato 'bomb' makes music)

Starting player gets potato
1. Rolls the die and draws a corresponding card
2. Pushes the potato button (primes it) 
3. Shouts a word which must be related to the term on the card
4. Throws the (hot) potato - direction was told on the card - to another player, who has to catch it
5. Player must respond with a word related to the last mentioned term and passes the potato further on - hopefully still making music
6. If the potato stops making music - that player should pick up a golden plastic chip
7. Game ends if anybody picks up the last chip

Content:
1 making music potato (with timer), 1 special die, 1 chip box, 30 chips, 1 card box, 56 cards, 1 rule sheet

similar approach compared with 'pass the bomb', but a bit easier to play
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
"Gamer Geeks, this game is going to rip you a new one, in a good way!" - Father Geek

"Overall, this game is just phenomenal. It brings a fresh type to the table top and hours of replayability. I cannot wait to be playing the finished product in my living room." - Demo Player Richard Nava

"I'm not a game player in general and I was surprised how much I got into (Specimen), playing the first time. I was PISSED when my flamethrower was going to be used against me. PISSED. And I was thrilled for the player next to me when she went for a move that was balls out ballsy…these guys are the real deal…FUN STUFF, FOLKS!" - Demo Player Rebecca L

SPECIMEN is a two-player card-driven board game that pits a group of human astronauts against a predatory extraterrestrial life form.

Each turn comprises four action rounds, where each player can perform one of several possible game actions. The Monster's movements are hidden from the Crew player behind a game screen, and the Monster's location is only revealed when the Monster attacks...or if the crew is lucky (or unlucky) enough to find it!

At the beginning of play, a mysterious specimen in the cargo hold has been let loose as a result of the damage caused by a meteor collision. This SPECIMEN is in an embryonic stage and evolves throughout the game, becoming more difficult to kill as the turns progress. In addition to attempting to kill the crew, the monster can also destroy sections of the ship, forcing the human players to divide their efforts between defense and structural repair. The monster can win the game by either killing all seven members of the crew or damaging enough sections of the ship that it breaks apart. Adding the destruction of the ship as an alternate victory condition allows for varied strategies on the part of both players. The human player can use tools to effect repairs, construct weapons and trackers to hunt down the monster…or ultimately blow up the ship if all seems lost.

The SPECIMEN player constructs his/her monster from a selection of 25 different attributes (offensive, defensive, or special abilities), providing new and different threats to the human player each game. This constantly changing monster, combined with 90 event cards driving game play, allows for infinite replayability. No two games should ever be the same.

Game description from the publisher:

In Catan Scenarios: Oil Springs settlers arriving on Catan will cry "Eureka!" as they discover black gold on the island for the first time. This resource brings great opportunity but also danger to the intrepid settlers as they make their mark on the island, claiming it for their own.

First, the good news: Oil can increase resource yields and allow a settlement to grow much faster than normal. The bad news is that players need to monitor the damage their oil use does to the environment and will eventually have to contend with global climate devastation which can result in all players losing the game!

Catan Scenarios: Oil Springs brings home the challenges and opportunities of the current global economy to players while adding more fun and complexity to the Catan experience.

Catan Scenarios: Oil Springs is offered both as a PnP game available on the Catan website, as well as a published edition.  The packaging states "revenue from the sales of the cardboard version of this scenario will be used to cover the expenses of graphics and printing.  Further profits will support Worldwatch's Transforming Cultures Project."
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Power Lines is a tile laying game for PiecePack in which players take turns placing tiles from a communal pool. One player is attempting to create rows, columns, or diagonals in which there are a majority of matching suits, while the opposing player is doing the same except for matching values.

When the 4x4 grid has been fully filled in, players score points for rows, columns, or 4-tile diagonals in which they have the largest matching set.

Although originally designed for the PiecePack game system, Power Lines is playable with a standard deck of playing cards.

Scene It? The Twilight Saga is new Scene It? game that has trivia, movie clips, and images from the first three Twilight movies: Twilight, New Moon, and Eclipse.

From the back of the Deluxe edition box:

Team Edward? Team Jacob?

Why choose!  Scene It? The Twilight Saga Deluxe edition features tons of clips, trivia questions and on-screen puzzles from all three blockbuster films, including The Twilight Saga: Eclipse!

With new mini-games and engaging group activities, you'll go beyond trivia and immerse yourself in the Twilight Saga.

Relive the passion, action and suspense of Twilight, The Twilight Saga: New Moon and The Twilight Saga: Eclipse all in on captivation package!

Exciting Group Activities
Classic Scene It? Puzzles!
Use the Remote to Solve On-Screen Puzzles!
Unique Puzzle Types!

Hosted by Twilight's Dr. Carlisle Cullen

For each game another Fun Park in Austria is selected as final destination and each player chooses a city where he wants to start. Then each player receives 5 city cards, these cities the player must visit on this way from start to finish. The roll of the die determines how many cities can be visited in one turn, a "6" allows to fly to any city. If a player passes through or reaches one of his cities, he may discard the corresponding card. The first player to reach the Fun Park without any city cards wins the game.

boardgame: Auf der Jagd nach dem Piratenschatz
description: Captain Sturmflut and his pirates Knollnase and Lücke flee the English fleet with the treasure they stole from the queen, which they want to hide on a lonely island. Monkey Kingo shows them the best hideout, but to become a real pirate Kingo has a lot to learn, especially to tie knots. In the game for up to 4 players the player will win who best remembers the story and is very good at knots.

Building towers, or rather build one tower by a joint effort. Who manages to place the roof or place the last block? The game is directed by cards. On a square basis area the triangular basis block is set up. The cards are stacked. In your turn you reveal the top building card - the cards determine which block must be placed with which hand. There are also cards offering free choice or change of turn order or cards demanding placement of a roof. If you can place a roof or the last block without tumbling the tower you receive a chip. If you collect three chips you win the game.

Puff foam marbles through archways

A somewhat different dexterity game - Pustekugel is played on the floor, you must puff the foam marble along a self-set-up track, either against another player or team or on your own against the clock. In a game against an opponent or opposing team only the cards showing three or less archways are used. You draw a card and then in turn players puff the ball through the archways. If you are the first to reach the starting point, you get the card. If you have the highest score from your cards after a pre-set number of rounds you win the game. If you race the clock you puff the marble along the track on your own, the fasted receives the card.

German card game. 31 cards. Each card has three types of train;
Personenzug = Local train
Eilzug = Fast train
D - Zug = Very fast train

Underneath each designation there is the name of a city which will have a railway station.

The game plays a little like Rummy in that you are trying to lay down sets of cities that are linked by one of the three train types. In other words, a train route.

First to play all their cards wins.

I think this must have required seriously good knowledge of trains and their destinations. I mean, is it likely that someone living in Hamburg would know a local train route around Munich? Perhaps it was only aimed at keen railway fans.

A quiz in the form of a fan, on the subject of soccer, 1000 questions and answers on Bundesliga, Weltmeisterschaft and Champions League. The cards are held by a screw and can be ideally taken on travels, the answers are always on the next page. The quiz can be played alone or by several players.

A quiz in the form of a fan, on the subject of dinosaurs, 1000 questions and answers sequenced by time periods. The cards are held by a screw and can be ideally taken on travels, the answers are always on the next page. The quiz can be played alone or by several players.

A lovely card game with almost Impressionist graphics.

Each card forms part of a set of four. There are 60 cards, so there are 15 sets to collect. Each set features a stage in your trip round the world, either a place or something in connection with travelling; for example - In the Far East or Means of Travelling. Players collect sets by asking each other for certain cards. If met with a "No" their turn ends. If successful, they can now ask someone else for a card and so it goes on.

A Dominion promo card to celebrate the 10th anniversary of Puerto Rico.

Text:




    
        Governor (Action - Cost 5 [coin])

    
    
        +1 ActionChoose one; you get the version in parentheses: Each player gets +1 (+3) Cards; or each player gains a Silver (Gold); or each player may trash a card from his hand and gain a card costing exactly 1 [coin] (2 [coin]) more.

    


Part of the Dominion series.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
A religious question and answer game, probably intended for Sunday Schools in East Germany in the fifties.
Contents: 20 Playing Boards + 96 Question and answer cards.

Up to 20 players get a board with 4 different chapters of the Bible. Every board is different.

The players (or teacher) take turns to read out one of the 96 Question cards. If another player thinks he recognises that scripture is one of his four chapters on his board, he can say the answer. If he is right he gets to keep the card and places it on the appropriate space on his board. If he is wrong, other players can guess if they want.
The first player to get all four chapters on his board covered, wins

Players need to make their way out of the snail’s shell and be the first to reach the salad. Two dice are rolled and placed on the snail’s antennae, the third you roll and then move your snail the sum of the die rolled and one of the antenna dice. The dice used are then placed on the antennae. On the green path you move forward, on the red backwards. You cannot enter closed spots, a doublet earns you another turn, on occupied spots you place your snail on top of others. When moving on you take along snails on top of yours and leave those below. On spots so marked you must change to the other path.

The heart of the game are 55 double-sided cards, thus presenting 110 important phrases, words or questions, specially geared to holiday needs and packed separately in a plastic box for easy transport. A ring-track on the board shows empty spots and spots with city names. Players roll the die and move their token, if it lands on a city name the player can try to translate one card. It the translation is correct he receives one chip. The first player to collect 5 chips wins the game.

The children carry their feeding bags through the zoo and look forward to meeting another animal. The feed tiles are laid out face down, the animals line up on the track. In his turn a player turns up a tile. If it is the favourite food of the animal first in line he can take the tile, otherwise he turns it down again. Then he places another animal in front of the line. When the forth food for any animal has been found and taken, this animal is taken off the board. When the third animal is taken off the game ends and the player with the most tiles wins.

The training box stands upright, the colour chips fall down, then the box is placed horizontally again. One learning table is chosen and is slid into the box. The child must find the colour corresponding to the correct solution for the task and move a colour chip with the magnetic pencil onto the solution spot of the board. To check for correct solution one pulls out the board and turns it over and slides it back in, the colour spots now must correlate with the colour chips.

The tree liana made up from two trunks and one cross-bar is set up and each player receives six animals: elephant, zebra, giraffe, monkey, crocodile and snake. Then in turn you use the symbol die: Depending on the symbol you must put one of your animals onto the bar or another animal, miss a turn, place an animal in a certain way, swap an animal with another player, or place an animal chosen by your neighbor. You first place animals underneath each other till the row nearly touches the surface, this must not be touched! Then you place animals anywhere in the chain and may use only one hand. You always have only one try to hand an animal, when the animal drops off you take it back.  When more animals drop you need only take two of them, the others go out of the game. When you could place all your animals you win the game.

Double sided promo technology card given out to Kickstarters of the 1st Edition as a bonus for the delay on production.

Elusive Victory
Permanent Technology
Research Cost: 12
Planets Required: Utopian and Prestige 
Prestige Points: Infinite
Effect: You win the game. No exceptions, not even for family.

Exclusive Victory
Permanent Technology
Research Cost: 5
Planets Required: None
Prestige Points: 0
Effect: If the game ends in a tie, you win the game. (Even if you're not one of the tied players)
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Kumbu is a memory game with an African theme and a twist, namely that the tiles have pictures on both sides. (Thus the name "Dubbelzijdig Memo", or double-sided memo.) When you play the game, you lay out the tiles, then take turns revealing two tiles to try to make pairs – but when you turn over a tile to reveal the other side, you don't turn it back if you fail to make a match. Thus, the hidden sides of the tiles keep changing throughout the course of the game. A player wins the game when he has the most tiles in the end.

Kumbu includes multiple variants.

Bassin’ Cards is an educational bass tournament card game for 1-5 anglers.  The object of the game is to catch the heaviest total weight of bass in a 1, 2, or 3 day fishing tournament.  Playing time varies with number of players & days fished.  Two players, fishing a two-day tournament, would take approximately 1 hour.
In this game, players place cards to set up areas to fish based on what lures are in their tackle boxes, and what presentations they can use.  As you play, you will learn the best techniques and fishing strategies under different external conditions.
The winner will be the one who can catch the heaviest stringer of fish when the tournament is over.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: A cork plateau, building blocks and constructions, which must be made up from the blocks - and yet neither  Bamboleo nor Make 'n' Break, but balance and dexterity, because you must balance the cork plateau on your back or your head or your knees and hold the plateau in balance for at least 2 seconds. You draw a card, place the plateau accordingly on head, back or knee and then the buildings blocks as shown on the card. When blocks tumble or the finished construction does not keep for 2 seconds, the card is put back beneath the stack. When the construction has been successfully completed, you keep the card. After the previously agreed number of rounds the winner is the player with most points on his cards.

Space Maze Rollercoaster is a mini expansion that contains 3 'Rollercoaster' cards which can be added to the deck of action cards in the base game. You should choose whether or not you want to use it before every game, because it changes gameplay considerably. If you do use it, make sure that in your board setup the relic tile is not on the edge of the board.

Effect: Take a tile at the end of any column the alien activating the card is in (up/down or left/right) and place it at the other end.  Now shove all tiles in that column one place further.  Aliens on tiles always move with them, even if they're moved to the other end of the board.

Adding the card to the base game changes the way you play in a way that it becomes more funny and chaotic and maybe a little less tactical. Therefore it's advised not to just add the card to your base game as if it's just one more action card.

'Space Maze Rollercoaster' was a free expansion when you bought the game at Essen 2011.

A surprise promo card originally given out in Essen 2011 with Ghost Stories: Black Secret. Steven Qi-Gal is an extra Wu-Feng only playable with the Ghost Stories: White Moon Expansion, because you need white Tao Dice or Moon Crystals to exorcise him. But he will let the Taoists save a villager when he comes into play and is replaced by a Buddha after he is exorcised.

This promo card was handed out at the Z-Man booth at Spiel 2011 in Essen.

The card reads: The King is attending SPIEL Essen.
In turn order, each player tries a Secret Amorous Liaison with the Queen until one player succeeds.

It is to be used with the base game as it has a normal F-back.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: The Vidar promo gives players one additional god to choose from.  When using this god's ability, a player may choose to use Elf tokens prior to a combat die roll. These used elf tokens count as two elf tokens each.

boardgame: Angelina Ballerina Dance With Me Game
description:
It's showtime! Angelina Ballerina is the star of the show and guess who she wants on stage too - YOU! Practice the moves step-by-step - every time you play it's a whole new ballet routine! When it's showtime, turn on the music and take center stage!

Players take turns spinning the spinner, collecting star cards and moving Angelina along the board rehearsing for her recital. When she reaches the It's Showtime! space each player has to try to perform the recital from memory. The player with the most star cards of the same color wins!

Promo item given out in Essen 2011 together with a preorder of Ghost Stories: Black Secret. The die is a replacement curse die the Wu-Feng player can use instead of the normal curse die. It has a Haunt, Demon, Curse, Skeleton, and Blank (no effect) side.

It was also available from the Repos stand at Essen 2014, with a dice bag and deluxe dice.

Like an Empire Deck or Sextet Bridge deck, the Blue Deck is a six-suited extension of a standard French-suited deck. The additional suits are blue stars and squids.

From the deck's website:

There is a long history of six-suited card decks. In 1895, Hiram Jones added Bullets and Crosses. Other designers have added Rackets and Wheels, Crowns and Anchors, or Valentines and Kites.
The Blue Sea Deck stands in that long tradition. Here the fifth and sixth suits are the blue suits of Stars and Squids. You can use the deck for playing Bridge with six, for making the odds in Poker go haywire, or for tossing card by card into a top hat.

The deck consists of six 13 card suits, 3 jokers, and a few information cards. It does not include rules or an actual squid.

In the early months of 1943, the United States and British Commonwealth forces had finally defeated Rommel and the axis armies of North Africa. The allied leaders were in disagreement on what the next step should be; invade Italy, the Balkans, or France. Stalin was desperate to open another front in Europe. Churchill wanted to invade Italy which he labeled “the soft underbelly of the Axis”. It was agreed that since no invasion of Northern France could be undertaken in 1943, that the invasion of Sicily would take place. The allied commanders were sure that invading Italy would all but destroy the Italians will to fight, and maybe the government and Mussolini would be overthrown.

The German high command was also aware that Italy’s support for the war was dwindling. German units that were needed for the Eastern front were sent to Italy to help defend against an allied attack and to police the Italian army in case of a coup. With most of Germany’s army and supplies on the Eastern front and a weakening ally in Italy, Germany is desperate to keep the allies out of Southern Europe.

The Invasion of Italy is an Axis & Allies style game that puts you in the seat of the Axis commanders who are racing to get their army into a position to defend Southern Europe, and Allied commanders who are ready to open another front in Europe to relieve the Russians on the Eastern front. Will you follow history as the allies and invade Sicily and then Italy, or will you try to take Rome first and severe the Axis army in Europe? Can the Axis hold out long enough with their limited supplies, or will Italy fall and leave Germany defending Italy…..ALONE?

The game features a map of Italy (36" x 48")(91 cm x 122 cm) divided into controllable territories. One player plays the side of the Axis controlling the Italian and German units defending Italy against the Allied Invasion. The other player controls the Allies who have 20 turns to take as many victory cities as possible before the Germans can reinforce Italy. Game uses a D12 combat system and pieces from the Axis & Allies series of games and is a variant of these games.

The Game comes with map , rules, and some units. You will need additional pieces to play the game.

A two player card game, in the similar style to Lost Cities, but different. It currently has no theme, and can as of now be played with two standard playing card decks, shuffled together.

The game involves the player in solitaire style card moving, card organization, thoughtful hand management, constant card battling with opponent, and fast quick turns, with drawing, recycling old cards, and discarding useless ones.  The game is designed to be very completive, use risk management, be tactically complex in options, and played through multiple rounds, with some games yielding multiple game points, so the far behind loser has a reason to fight with a bad hand.

Players involve themselves in gameplay in which they draw cards during their turn, maintaining hand sizes of 10, and play those cards down on differing piles, to accumulate points, matching the suits.  However, the opposing player is constantly battling for victory as well, with his piles point totals.  Players also discard and recycle cards onto the various piles.  Many cards have special attributes which mix up the play.  First player to reach an objective of 5 game points, (added from various round points), is the winner.

A game for all friends of dinosaurs. The game holds 600 questions and answers on the subject, players try to win colourful dinosaur disks. A special point of interest are the short descriptions of more than 70 dinosaur varieties, including info text and 6 questions. The rule gives 2 game variants.

A Valdora promo tile (for Essen 2011)

The special board is placed next to the game board. As long as it is next to the game board it can be claimed by discarding different-colored craftsman tiles. Immediately after another player has carried out "Purchase equipment or commissions" action in a city, you may, out of turn, immediately buy the topmost commission or equipment card in that city (card costs are as normal). The game then continues as normal with next player whose turn was interrupted taking his turn.

The topic chips for human - natural - heavenly - terrestrial are sorted and laid out as stated in the rules, as are the randomly chosen and shuffled obstacle tiles. These obstacles can only be mastered by discarding of topic chips. The questions cards are sorted in order of the topics, but if so desired can be shuffled.

In your turn you are the questioner, you draw the front card and name the topic, the facts, the questions and all answers. Then all players in turn choose and name one of the answers. Then the correct answer is read. If you answered correctly you can either move your pawn or take a corresponding topic chip from stock. If you reach a face-down topic chip first, you get it. If all answer wrong, the questioner gets a chip. If you answer correctly "all wrong", you move one step and get a chip. A spot containing an obstacle tile may only be entered if you can discard 2 corresponding topic chips. The rules also provide for players who have dropped back very far.

A special expansion tile from the Spiel 2011 Essen convention.

The first player to fill up his 6-space enclosure gets the Catta enclosure and places 3 coins from the bank on it. If it is the owners turn he can skip this turn but has to pay one coin from the enclosure to the bank. If there is no coin left on the enclosure he cannot skip his turn. If at least one coin is left on the enclosure by the end of the game the player gets one additional scoring point.

www.yali-ron.com/game1.php

It is very complicated to find a parking place in the big city and even more amusing when other factors are on your way...
This is a comic card game, chalanging you to be the first one to park 4 cars by overcoming the difficulties.
So what are you waiting for, the keys are inside...

During the game the players need to collect a car card with a card of matching keys (matching the type of the car and its color). Meanwhile the players may use other cards to steal parked cars (with the thief card) from others parking lot, unless a guard card is used as an encounter maneuver.

From the publisher site (in Hebrew):
מאוד מסובך להחנות מכוניות בצפיפות הבלתי אפשרית של ימינו. 
אך מסובך ומשעשע יותר כאשר ישנם גורמים אשר מנסים להפריע לכם לעשות זאת ... 
נראה אתכם מתמודדים עם ה"קשיים" המשעשעים ומחנים ראשונים 4 כלי רכב בחניה. 
משחק קומי על חווית החניה וכל הקשור בה. 
משחק קלפים מקורי, משעשע וממכר. 
אז למה אתם מחכים? המפתחות בפנים!
Coney Island: Giant Wheel, a promotional postcard for Coney Island first available at Spiel 2011, replaces one of the two existing Grand attraction tiles of size four.

boardgame: The Castles of Burgundy: 1st Expansion – New Player Boards
description:
4 double-sided player boards, numbered 10a–10h.

These boards are part of the Spielbox magazine issue 05/2011.

Rules:
In the original game, you have to lay all tiles in the princedom next to at least one already laid-out tile. Now, not only do you have to place them as just described, but in addition, the tiles always need to be connected - directly or indirectly (i.e. by the same color) with at least one castle.

In other words: If you add a new tile, it must lie either directly next to a castle - or at least be connected with a castle by the same color (as the tile); a different color is not allowed.

This rule doesn't apply to new castles; they may be added as before.

At any time, for the cost of both dice actions and 5 victory points, a player may retrieve a previously set-aside (that was unclaimed/purchased from the main board in a previous round) Castle tile (Green or Black-backed) and place it in their reserve.

Objective: Try to (visit and) solve as many accidents as possible - collecting points/acting by turns

1 There are accidents - spread all over the board 
2 If your die shows a 'five' - your car may start
3 Choose a route on which you can solve these accidents as quick as possible
3 Game ends if the last accident was followed up

Soccer quiz, the winner is the player who scores 25 points first. In turn players are asked a question, for a correct answer one scores a point. In case of a wrong answer all players can try to answer, the fastest player with the correct answer wins the point.

Objective: Be the first to move your car over the finish line
(field 90)
Simple 'roll and move' GDR game learning traffic signs and rules
as typical for comparable products: Individual meeples (cars) and detailed coloured map

A game all about betting. One player is elected to be the bookmaker. He offers odds as he sees fit and the players then bet on the race. A vinyl record is provided and, when played, will be a commentary of a race. Depending where the needle settles on at the start, you can get different races with the same runners. The box claims it can be different every time. The other side of the record has a different race, just for variety. The record is rather nice, what would be later called a picture disc.

Objective:
Be the first to reach target line
Typical 'roll and move' game before WW I. 
Very nice drawings/printing and  - of course - pewter pawns
Rules inside cover

Artist:
Emil Heinsdorff

remark: This game belongs to the series:
"Scholz künstlerische Spiele" - 2 different product codes

Carrancas is a board game inspired by the Brazilian colonization process. In 1501, some men tried to explore the continental territory of Brazil using the San Francisco river to get inside the continent. These man were explorers and warriors, fighting against diseases, native tribes and the wild nature.

In Carrancas, the players assume the position of these men, going to the uplands of the rivers with manpower and food, bringing down gold, leather and silver.

Carrancas were the ancient spiritual totems used by the natives across the San Francisco river, to bring peace, cure diseases and all kind of help needed. This totems were used in the boats and villages. This Totems may win a war.

A nice and handy gimmick vaguely looking like a smiling face is filled with cardbord discs on which questions and answers are printed. All discs are shuffled and inserted into the gimmick. Then the child turns the "nose" on the front of the "Questioner" until the question can be read in the window. Then the child answers, using one of the choices given, and remembers the number next to his chosen answer. When he lifts the Question Mark, the number visible underneath shows the correct answer. The questions are sorted into three different grades of complexity, marked with 1, 2 or 3 stars. The question must be positioned exactly in its window to have the correct answer appear in the answer window. A nicely done and well working quiz and learning game, working well as a solitary game.

This quiz game is played in two rounds. In the first round the theme cards are used, the theme cards show names of historical persons or titels of wellknown works of arts or literature. The players must name the right time for either life of the person or the time when the work was generated. If the time is named correctly, the player may take a token from the board. In the second round event- and person-cards are used. Now a series of events must be ordered chronologically, or the players must name a year within the lifespan of a certain person. The game ends when all tokens are taken off the board.

In this game, players alternate taking the role of the fish, and the other try to find the optimum pattern to catch him. Players get 100 practice casts trying to discover the best pattern before the 20 cast tournament. After each tournament, players are scored for how they did.  Players fish in real time at their own pace. 
After everyone has a chance at being the elusive bass, the scores are added up and a winner is determined.

Clownpocalypse is a card game for 2-4 players, set in a world ravaged by evil clowns.  One player plays the Clowns: their goal is to abduct the remaining humans and force them into the Circus.  All the other players play Survivors, who roam the blasted wastes in a pitiful search for food, salvageable material, and safety... and who try to take out as many Clowns as possible along the way.

In Clownpocalypse, each player playing a human Survivor leads a Team.  At the start of the game your Team is just a single Survivor, but a big part of the game is rescuing other Survivors and attempting to lead them to safety. Each Survivor you shepherd to safety at game's end is worth a victory point, but having a large Team during the course of gameplay can create problems: the larger your Team, the more attention they attract from the Clowns.

It also gets tougher to feed them. In classic survival horror fashion, you start the game with only a limited number of Rations, and you'll need to use your quickly dwindling supply to feed each member of your Team every turn.  If your Team gets too large too fast, you're going to have trouble, and then you're going to need to do a hard moral calculus: do you let someone starve? Do you cannibalize your own Team? (Each of these choices has their own pros and cons.)

After you've fed your Team, you get the option to Travel. Traveling is the main way you move through the game deck, uncovering resources and, eventually, the Way Out.  There will always be three cards in the gameplay area displaying three possible resources to Travel to.  Those resources could be Locations, Rations, Salvage, Weapons, or other Survivors, and part of your strategic thinking in the game will involve assessing which resources your Team really needs--you can only attempt to get one per turn, leaving the rest on the table for your rivals.

And of course, there's danger.  The Clowns want to stop you from reaching those resources. So, each turn, the player playing the Clowns places one Clown card, face down, in front of each resource on the table.  This is where the game becomes a battle of wits.  The Clowns have some cards that are stronger than others, and so they need to carefully choose where they place their best cards.  Which resource on the table is the most valuable to the player whose turn it is?  Is that the one that the Clowns should defend the strongest?  Or should they bluff, placing a weaker Clown there in the event that the player decides to grab up a lesser resource, in the hope of avoiding confrontation?  Being able to predict your opponent's strategic choices is an important part of this game.

Once you've declared what resource you're travelling to (or opted to stay put, which has its own risks), the Clowns flip the card they uses to defend that resource, and a confrontation ensues.

Clownpocalypse contains 64 cards (Clowns, Survivors, Weapons, Salvage, and Locations), a set of counters to represent Rations, plus some other goodies in an attractive, sturdy box.  Clownpocalypse is hilarious, disturbing, challenging, and rewarding: you're going to love it.

In the adventure, you and your friends (tha' be players later on) take on the roles of legendary privateers, shrewd buccaneers and other kinds of bloodthirsty scallywags. All of whom are tied together by the code and honour amongst pirates.
Both code and honour will be betrayed multiple times as the players plot and make mischief for and against each other. One adventure takes approximately one to two hours, including all preparations and planning, such as shuffling the cards, creating characters and making up the premise. The game is playable with just the cards, but you might find the game easier to follow if you use paper to make notes and count the crosses in the Davy Jones Counter. The game is all about having fun. Push your characters into complicated and fun situations. Curse like a scallywag. Cheat fate, act like a legend, and if the inevitable cold grave calls you - go with a bang.

Each player has a character. After creating the characters, you may use the cards and the adventure-o-matic to create a grand,
challenging adventure. All scenes start from a plot twist. The twists might be positive, helpful, funny or they might come in the shape of a challenge. Players respond to challenges by using cards from their hand. A player may play as many cards of the same suit as he likes, but the sum of their values must be at least equal to the challenge card’s value in order to beat the challenge. If the character is unable to resolve a challenge with the cards in hand, the situation might call for a betrayal. A challenge from another character’s action can kill a character, if the challenge winning player calls for it. However, challenges between characters do not need to be deadly: a character might try to seduce another or steal his hat.

Pirates never betray each other, or the pirate code, Well... at least if it’s not a matter of life and death ... Or if it’s not about love ... Or if there is no gold in it ... Or… well, you get the picture. A player calls for a betrayal, and asks for a suit. Even if the characters cannot die directly from the challenges, there is a price for failure and betrayal. Sooner or later the cold arms of the grave will welcome all pirates of the sea. There should be 12 empty slots in each character sheet. These slots are used to keep score of the character’s failures and betrayals. Each time the character fails a challenge, two X’s are added to the counter.
Each time the character betrays the others, one X is added to the counter.

And each time the character gets one or more X’s - a card is drawn. If the value of the card is equal or less than the number of X’s on the character sheet, the character’s days are numbered and he is doomed.If a character already has 12 X’s on the sheet, he is immediately doomed, when he gets the 13th mark.
A doomed character will die in any of the next three scenes. Players should not feel too attached to a doomed character. He might look pale or even ghostly or see omens of his death for he knows that the day is his last.

When and how does the game end?
The game should end if
• Adventure has reached its conclusion,
• You reach agreed time limit (Professional pirates recommend using 2 hour time limit)
• Players are too tired (of drinking Rum) to play on.

Add more building fun to every game of Creationary!

Expand your Creationary game fun with 2 new categories: Toys and Sports! This booster pack for your Creationary game includes 20 new Creationary cards, 5 green elements and 5 orange elements.

•Includes 20 all-new Creationary cards and elements

In #OccupyBoardwalk, players take the role of the 1% trying to retain control of the board against increasing demonstrations against their rule. The goal is to be the player with the most money at the end of the game, assuming the 99% don’t successfully occupy too much of your precious property! If that happens, everyone loses.


    OccupyBoardwalk is an Uncooperative Game for 3 or More Players. It just requires a copy of Monopoly and a book. (It can be any book, but we suggest Das Capital, Road to Serfdom or a pocket copy of the Constitution).  


Each round, the public expects the 1% to pay another 1000 dollars of your fortunes towards the public good. To do this, the player with the Top Hat opens the book to a page an puts any amount of money into the book secretly. They then flip to another page and pass the book to the next player. When a player gets the book, they can’t flip backwards in the book to see what other players have payed. Once each player has had an opportunity to put money into the book, the first player turns the book over and shakes it over the board. Collect the money that falls out, count it and put it under public parking.

Remember two things: The player with the most money left at the end of the game wins. Also, the public expects the 1% to pay another $1000 every turn. So the expectations of the public about the amount of money in public parking increases every turn. There are a few other twists and turns involving the removal of protestors from properties, and the use of Chance and Community Chest cards to get bailed out when things get rough. The full rules are available in the attachments or at the link provided in the link section

BACKWORD is a new, fun and different wordgame.

The game is divided into two fases. All players draw 8 letter-cards and make a word. Afterwards the players atempt to guess each others words, by gruadually show the letters - one by one - from behind!

The game is for adults, but is simple enough for larger children. Also possible to play in teams.
2-5 players
Age: 15+

"Hamsterdam is the best dam game around."

"The flood waters are piling up and the noblest of creatures are all that remain to protect us."

How to play: 
Create a "dam" made up of a line of cards numbered 2 through 12 and distribute your hamster cards (20 regular + 2 special) on top of the numbered cards.  Take turns rolling the dice to see which spot in the damn is weakened.  If a hamster is on top of that number, that hamster is used to plug to the hole and is placed to the side in a reserve pile to come back into play in the next round.  If a hamster is not on that number, a flood token is placed there until the end of the round, at which time it can be removed by taking a hamster card permanently out of play.  If a flood token is already located on a number that is rolled, another flood token is placed in a special pile of tokens belonging to that player which cannot be removed.  As flood tokens pile up, your stock of hamster saviors dwindles, and flood tokens pile up all the faster.  The game ends when any player accumulates 10 flood tokens (counting both those in the permanent pile and those currently on the dam).  The winner is the player with the fewest flood tokens.  In the case of a tie, the player with the most remaining hamster cards wins.

Play Ball! is a dice game consisting of 3 dice.  The dice are wooden, six-sided and consist of the following faces:

Red - Hit, 2 Base, 3 Base, Out, Out, Out
Blue - Hit, Hit, 2 Base, Out, Out, Out
Green - Hit, Hit, 3 Base, Home Run, Out, 2 Outs

The rules are simple and consist of the following (taken from the rules sheet):

1. Roll out 3 Cubes and use Baseball Rules of Play.
2. Always read Cubes in same order.
      RED-BLUE-GREEN
3. Tally runs on Score Pad for each inning.
4. When 3 Outs are made, player passes Cubes to other team or player.

The contents of the game are:

One small, plastic canister to hold the contents
One 2" by 3" rules sheet/score pad
Three dice (Red, Blue,Green).

The year is 2164. Mankind has self-actualized enough to realize that we are creatures of violence. Centuries of denying one of our base instincts has not led to a more civilized society. Technological advances have given unprecedented scales of damage potential to our conflicts. In 2153, the Council of Restraint was formed in response to the particularly devastating aftermath of a conflict between the United African Republic and Pacific Rim Conglomerate. Three years later the Conflict Resolution Accords were ratified. Now when conflicts arise, the involved states or ideological groups can settle the dispute in a manner that mitigates widespread destruction. Large, mechanized military machines meet to do battle of epic proportion. These battles are waged in controlled arenas to contain the violence and damage. Armored Terran Systems Corporation manufactures base machines called Mechs and combatants configure their Mech with armaments and options in preparation for combat.

The Earth Shook is a game where each player assumes the role of a large mechanized military class robot. Where man once waged war across the globe on epic proportions, machines now resolve the conflict in controlled arenas. Build your machine, defend your ideology, and destroy the others. Be the last machine standing on the field of battle; to the victor go the spoils of war.

Players each receive a basic identical Mech from the factory. They have a set amount of points that they can use to buy modules for the Mech. These modules adjust the ratings for Evasion, Shields, Armor, Speed, Maneuverability, Weapon Damage, Rate of Fire, and more. Once the modules have been purchased, the Mech stats are adjusted to reflect the module choices and teh game can commence.

Game play is similar to RoboRally or Wings of War in that each player will select 6 Move/Maneuver actions for their Mech each turn. In this manner, a turn is played out in six rounds as the Mechs execute their programmed moves. Can you out-maneuver your opponent? Do you risk an all out frontal attack. Each player will approach the battle differently, hoping to draw upon the strengths of their configured Mech.

Build & Battle. The game is on...

Knights and ghost hold a race on the sides of the box, in the bottom of the box the castle is put over one of the quiz pages, windows of the castle show 4 correct and 4 wrong answers to the question on top of the page. A trump and guessing game on 32 horse races, the cards show a number range, the name of the race, the size and weight of the animal, the colours of the coat and the age of the race. The cards are dealt, in his turn a player rolls the die and depending on the roll he chooses a very big or a very small number from the information on his cards, the next player answers with his biggest or smallest number in the same category. The player with the highest or lowest number respectively wins both cards. If one player has run out of cards, the player with the most cards wins. Packed in a belt bag, with key ring horse pendant.

Memeopoly is a fun social drinking game which isn't complicated and doesn't require you to memorize or agree upon the rules.
The object of the game is simply to have fun with your friends and have a few drinks. You are able to quantify a victory by choosing to play with certain rules.

				
				
					How to play:
         Draw a card.
         Do what the card says.
         Discard the card.
         Next Player’s turn.
    


The game ends when you run out of cards or find a special “Win” card near the end of the deck.
The text on the cards range from taking drinks, listing categories of objects, attempting physical feats, speaking in funny accents and deck manipulation.

There are several different ways to play including mechanics of hand management, point accumulation and just playing for the sake of playing the different cards.

				
				
					Game details:
    Players 3-10.
    Age Range: Drinking age and upwards.
    Duration: 30min to 2hrs depending on player count and number of cards.
    Complexity: Easy to play, no complicated rules. Remove certain cards to customize gameplay.
    Decks: Available in decks of 52 cards, every card is unique with a unique rule. Currently two decks are available.


				
				
					More info:You can try the game, download the print and play version of the game for free, or purchase a deck from games official website.

description: Each child has ist own picture of a farmyard, the missing animals are only visible in contours. To make it easy to find the missing animals, the conturs are coloured. All 24 animals are put into a bag, and players take turns to pull out an animal from the bag. On each card 6 animals are missing, players must pay close attention which animals are pulled out of the bag, since the animals have different colors on each picture. On one the duck is red, on the next one yellow, and so on. Only animals in the correct color count, who is the first to fill his picture with all the missing animals, wins.

This in an educational game from Brasil. The child attempts to match images of fruits and vegetables by combining two beautifully illustrated cards. Each card contains 50% of the object and 50% of the name.

The cards fit together like two halves of a puzzle to form a complete image, so the child learns by trial and error as the game progresses.

A successful match helps the child learn the word for the object and teaches the child how the fruit or vegetable appears in the natural world.

Includes 22 cards, rules, and a storage insert.

A solitaire option is available.

Sci-fi miniature wargame set in a cruent XII century universe. Players engage in battles on planet surfaces commanding armies belonging to several warring parties, such as Terrax (humans), Torek and Sarmath.

The game is designed for quick matches which usually does not exceed one hour in duration. Battle resolutions are calculated using just 6-sided dice. Victory is achieved by fulfilling randomly-selected objectives which are defined at the start of the game (one main objective and several secondary ones). During a game players alternate in activating one unit each until all of them have moved and a game turn is completed. Each unit can execute up to 2 orders with an activation, while elite units can execute up to three actions with a single activation.

Colonial Wars is played using 20 mm custom miniatures (unpainted), available in thematic blisters.

The 4th Indian Division assault on Halfaya Pass during Operation Battleaxe, June 15-16 1941


Halfaya Pass is another small GSI offering. The game focuses on tactical level combat and maneuver during Operation Battleaxe prior to the Crusader operation that lifted the seige of Tobruk. This is a stand alone game and does not need any other GSI title to be played.  It is slightly larger than Hafid Ridge in terms of the number of units and can be played in a relatively short time.The rules are modeled around the seven battlefield operating systems which make them easily understood and easy to learn.

This battle will provide a challenge to both players.  The British will find that they need to be very aggressive to push through the anti-tank defense.  The Germans have to simply hang on.  Either way it's a cliff hanger.  The British Matilda's are the queen of the battlefield, until they meet the 88's.  Obstacle play is important and significant.

Game play is excellent for solitaire play and moves along very quickly. The game is specifically designed to play in a day.

The Commonwealth forces were made up of 3 infantry battalions and a squadron and a half of Matilda tanks, heavy but undergunned and underpowered.

This game focuses on company / platoon combat and combined arms use. The time scale is either 1 or 2 hours per turn and each hex is 330 meters, depending on the game.

This game comes with:

    (2) 11x17 Color Map Sections
    (1) Color Order of Battle Card
    Approximately 200 Double-sided and Die Cut Counters

description:
Find answers to eternal questions or get insight into burning issues such as life, the universe and everything, whether the earth will exist in 2013, or what really happened to Atlantis.  Quidnam is not just a game; it’s a mirror on life and the totality of human existence.

Quidnam is a unique card deck that can be used for contemplation and illuminating solitaire meditation or in a groupthink party game.  The heart of Quidnam is the deck of 64 insightful cards developed from fundamental principles of human existence using Set Theory.  Each card has lore and art to provide verbal and visual suggestions as to their meaning.  There are 4 suits of 16 cards.  These suits represent the 4 planes of existence - physical,  biological, cognitive, and social.  Each plane (suit) has 4 unique elements and a color; each card in the suit is composed of a combination of those four elements from zero to four.   The mix of elements is shown on each card.

Meditation.  To meditate, simply decide on a topic, shuffle the deck, deal some cards using a pattern or method to your liking, and study the result.  You can do this alone or with family and friends.  Some patterns for reading are shown in the action shots.  You can create your own patterns and methods.  The topic is up to you.

The Quidnam deck can be used to play the Groupthink game.  This party game is best played with at least 4 players; the more the better.  A question or issue is chosen at the beginning of play.  Play consists of one or more rounds.  For each round, one player serves as a judge.  The role of judge rotates from round to round.  Shuffle the deck and deal some cards using a pattern or method that the group likes.  All players look at these cards and develop a secret reading which they write down and give to the judge.  The judge picks the best reading and the winner gets a point.  Several rounds should be played.  The player with the most points wins.

Important note.  Quidnam forms the foundation for the exciting game of Totality.

Totality also shows how the set theoretical design maps the cards to the I Ching and Zodiac.

"Collect, Stretch & Destroy!"

Battle Bands is a collectible elastic band game where players build armies and take turns flicking them at a board. When two opposing bands touch, they "battle" using the corresponding character cards to see who wins. The game ends when all players have depleted their armies,and the points are tallied based on where their bands are on the board.

The elastic bands are shaped like different creatures, and come in red, blue and green. Each elastics' corresponding card has a strength level for the creature, and players build armies up to a certain level (for example, if they are "brawling, each player's army will consist of creatures totaling a strength of 50).

In addition, each player may add one black elastic band to their army. These black creatures have special abilities which will affect the board.

The game draws heavily from Magic: The Gathering for theme, and from Heroscape for building your army of bands.

Objective:
be the first to cross finish line

Simple 'Roll and Move' game, with - of course - nice drawings and individual meeples (hunter)

German hunting organisation (see sticker)

Players play as snakes competing to collect eggs scattered across the board. Every time you eat an egg, your snake grows longer. Watch out, though; snakes can eat each other's tails, so a long snake is a tempting target!

Points are awarded for eating eggs and other snake's tails. A balance of both is usually needed to win.

description: 20 animal cubes are placed within the board, white side up. In his move a player determines a starting animal by placing his chip on the cube. The colour die determines the category, speed, breeding time, size, weight or age, the Symbol die determines whether the chosen animals must be stronger or weaker in the category than the starting animal. All players in turn take one cube till all pass, then first the active player and then all others compare the colour codes of the chosen animals - for correct choices they move 1 spot forward, for a wrong choice 1 spot back. Dog spots on the scoring scale earn a point for actions, rabbit actions earn one point for the active player if he finds out which animal cube was removed behind his back.

The playerboard was available at Essen fair 2011.

description:
A game to help 'Tiny Tots' to recognise names and objects. A question sheet is laid over the mechanics. On the left there are 3 pictures per line, making 12 pictures altogether per sheet. On the right are 12 boxes with the names of the objects. A child must touch an object with the lead and then touch the name. If correct, bulbs will light up.
When played competitively, the first one to get a pre-set number of questions right, wins.

A Monopoly clone set in the coastal town of Wilhemshaven, Germany. The game has sponsored by a local radio station and  by various Wilehmshaven entrepreneurs, the players have to acquire and trade company shares instead of streets.

The penalties for players ending their move on an already occupied field only increase with the number of company shares with identical colors; the concept of additional buildings such as hotels been dropped. Instead, this game features profit and loss statements that are triggered by certain fields. They change share values, resulting in players paying money into or getting money from a money pool. There are also pool cards triggering the profit and loss statements of shares of one color.

Middle-earth Play-By-Mail is an award-winning strategic game set the world of J.R.R. Tolkien's Middle-earth. In it, you take on the role of the heroes (and villains) of Middle-earth, leading armies, undertaking perilous adventures, governing your cities and towns, and making all the decisions that affect your empire.

Middle-earth Play-By-Mail is a team game, where you work together as part of either the Free Peoples in defending the innocent against Sauron's hatred, or as a Dark Servant, a hand of Sauron.

Middle-earth Play-by-Mail is a turn-based game. This means that rather than playing online, you have the luxury of time in which to consider and plan your next move, before submitting your new orders.
A simple roll and move game. If you land on an instruction, you must obey it. This was the first Spears game featuring Tufty the Squirrel.
A simple race game. Throw dice and move, and obey any instructions

The 2nd editon of COMBAT STORM is a turn-based miniature wargame where players use green and tan plastic army men as their game pieces and a large playing area, such as a living room floor or large table as their battlefield!

What started out originally as an expansion pack, quickly turned into a full-fledged new edition packed full of exciting new rules, soldier classes, vehicles, and gameplay mechanics.

There were too many cool new things we wanted to explore, that an expansion pack just wouldn't do it justice.

Stellar Prospector is a light game for 3-5 players vying for the chance to run a start-up franchise that prospects rare elements from stars in their planetary system.  The first candidate franchisee to complete five specific delivery missions of specific rare elements to particular planets wins the right to run the franchise (and thus wins the game).

Players start the game with a small amount of money but no ships,  pilots or fuel.  In order to prospect and deliver rare elements, players must hire pilots and buy ships and fuel.  Ships may be bought and pilots hired either at auction or from the shop.  Higher level pilots and ships have a better chance of success but cost more money.  Players make more money either by prospecting and delivering rare elements or bumming around the home base planet doing odd jobs.

Players must learn which stars contain what rare elements through trial-and-error missions and taking note of other players' routes and deliveries.

So players must balance observation with action, knowledge with uncertainty in their race to complete their missions, because star mining is a risky business, with pilot error or ship failure resulting in a fiery end to both.

Within the shadows of the wilds, events are set in motion that will bring the area’s powers into a desperate clash by which their fates in the west will be determined. As a returned hero prepares to enact an ancient ritual to foil the plots of dragons, the Skorne Empire resumes its march for conquest and the beleaguered United Kriels must outfox old enemies in their continued fight for the survival of their race.

HORDES: Domination heralds the next exciting chapter of the HORDES saga. Set loose the savagery of war with:
• New troops to bring to the fight, including ten new warlocks and the debut of the mighty battle engines.
• New narrative fiction, including compelling stories for each HORDES faction.
• A painting and modeling guide to help you ready your forces for battle.
• New Theme Force lists for each new warlock, which allow you to create armies based on specialized forces found in the HORDES world.

description:
Trivia party game featuring very famous advertisements and you have to guess the product. The guest who gets the most right, wins.

Each player receives a double-sided card with 18 clues to famous adverts in Britain in the 50's. Altogether there are 8 visual clues and ten written clues.
Party game, a simple trivia game about Britain's first TV soap opera. The guest with the most trivia facts about this programme, wins.

A party game for the more observant guests. Everyone gets to see up to 12 pictures. Then, more pictures, with subtle differences are left where guests can see them, and they must write down what is missing from the second picture version. Whoever gets most right answers durin the evening, wins.

Power Grid: Theme Park (Funkenschlag: Freizeitpark) is a promotional card for Power Grid that was available for a donation at the 2F-Spiele booth during Spiel 2011.  This card is also part of the BGG Promo Card Set.

The card is auctioned to the players immediately after it has been drawn. It counts as an additional city for its owner and not as a power plant.

A party game for trivia enthusiasts. Volunteer guests are asked trivia questions and the guest who gets the most correct, wins.
Questions and answers provided by the Encyclopedia Britannica.
The box says it is related to the television show, but, as it is just quiz questions, the company could have said it was related to hundreds of other TV game-shows!

Note the odd title - not a pound or dollar sign at all.

A massive social-trading puzzle game designed for BGG.con 2011. The game was created using 5,000 unique square cards, each with a symbol on each edge and a number in the middle.

Each player is given four cards. The object is to trade cards and then arrange four cards into a highly-valued set, where points are awarded based on matching the colors, fillings, and shapes of the symbols on interior and exterior edges.

In Masterplan, players take turns placing houses onto a 8x8 square grid. Houses can be placed at the sides or in the corners of the squares.

Whenever three houses are placed in such a way that a 4th house could make a row of 4 (no matter in which colour combination), a green park piece is placed where the 4th house could be placed. Players who have adjacent pieces to these new placed park(s) score at this moment. If a house is placed on the center of the sideline directly adjacent to this space (2 points) or at the corner (if diagonally adjacent, valued 2 points) or only on the adjacent space but at another line or corner (1 point).

Whenever a full square of houses could be build (3 houses creating an "L") a tower is placed onto the fourth space. These towers score only at the end of the game, depending on the majority around them. (Same scoring as parks, but you don't actually score the points. Whoever would score the most points for a given Tower receives 2 points.

Games ends after all houses are built and all towers are scored.
