description:
Other kids' games are all such a bore!
They've gotta have rules and they gotta keep score!
Calvinball is better by far!
It's never the same! It's always bizarre!
You don't need a team or a referee!
You know that it's great, 'cause it's named after me!
—The Calvinball theme song

Calvinball is a game played by the characters of the classic comic strip Calvin and Hobbes by Bill Watterson.

The goal of Calvinball is to round the circuit of bases in sequence while in the possession of the required Calvinball for each base.  One time successful around the bases wins the game.  Opponents will attempt to halt other Player’s advancement while simultaneously trying to reach their own sequential base with their Calvinball (it may or may not be the same ball).  Scattered around the field are trees, rocks, a stream, sports equipment and other items to hinder and assist players in their efforts to win the game

A Chess-like move and capture game played on an 8 x 8 board which includes terrain, which enables ambushes, has swamps to avoid and forts into which retreats may be conducted, but which may also be besieged.

boardgame: The Game of the Red Pig
description:
Game circa 1930's from Parker Brothers.

This is sort of a spin and move game where depending on what number you get from the spinner you move forward that number of places on the board. There are certain places on the board with special letters on them. Those spaces allow you to move forward extra spaces. Landing on places with Black dots make you move back some number of spaces. First to finish wins!

Box says:  Explore the great city of Paris from the comfort of your home.  A strategy-travel game, for 2-6 players.  #7471, 1974 Gameophiles Unlimited, Inc. 16 Pine St., Morristown, NJ 07960.  Patent Pending.

Roll the die and move.  Land on special squares and pick cards that move you to different places like the Louvres Museum or the Eiffel Tower, Arch of Triumph, Church of Sacre Couer etc. Enter the city from Orly Airport, The Palace at Versailles, Longchamps or Colombey. See the needed number of places in the city to win the game.

boardgame: ZOMBIES!  You Won't Survive The Night.
description:
Well, it's finally happened.  It might have been a plague, some sort of crazy military experiment, or just a freak mutation of some virus; but it doesn't matter, the dead are walking and they are hungry!

ZOMBIES!  You Won't Survive The Night is a Zombie-themed card game where you and your friends make up a party of survivors who are doing their best to simply survive.  The only problem...  They won't!  You cannot survive this night!  Everyone WILL DIE!!!

While you and your friends are trying to make it, you will go into houses and businesses looking for food and weapons.  On occasion, you might just find a Zombie...  Of course, dealing with the Zombies in a large group (Up to 10 players) might seem easy at first but eventually you lose people... and when you do, they become Zombies too!

Play doesn't end when you die, your priorities simply change.  Once you die and become a Zombie, your goal is simple: Kill all the living and eat their brains!

Will you be the last to survive?  Will you be the last Zombie standing?  Each play-through will be different in this game of tactics and chance.

Objective: collect/gain as many tiles/chips with quality fishes 
as possible
- moving by turns
1. players place their meeples on the board (circle) - youngest one starts 
2. turn a tile (fish is shown)- try to catch it by
3. rolling the dice - move your meeple (forewards or backwards)
4. or move the octopus - to block the loot 
5. pick up the loot - if accessible
6. flip over another tile/fish - later on 2 tiles at once
7. game ends if no tiles are available 
Player with high score (most valuable fishes) wins

Giveaway from the "Spiel 2011 in Essen (Germany)".

Contains the follwing gaming material:

1 "Divine Favor" tile for "Hecate"
2 Hecate columns
6 cloth bags for the 5 player colors and one for hades (expansion)

Note: You need the "Cyclades: Hades" expansion in order to use Hecate.

Divine favors for Hecate:
- Receive for Free: A Priestess card.
- Only once per turn: For 2 GP, place columns on two different islands, regardless of who owns them. These two islands are now connected by an underground passage. This passage can be used to move troops from one of these islands to the other without fleets to carry them. If Hecate comes back into play, you may move either or both columns for 2 GP. Otherwise, these columns stay in place.

A micro-expansion for 1830: Railways & Robber Barons featuring one city, Chattanooga. It is  in honour of Mark Derrick's Chattanooga Railgaming Challenge, held every year in Chattanooga.

This expansion adds one city to a less congested corner of the map, giving the players an incentive to build into otherwise quite empty area.

This was a give-away item with purchase in Spiel '11.

The Beggar is a new Action card for Jerusalem.

Just add the card to the basic Action card deck to play with it.

When you play this Action card, place it under one of your squire who's inside one area of the gameboard. That Squire will become a beggar for the rest of the game. Each time an opponent places one (or more) squire inside that area, must give 1 silver Bezant to the beggar.
Note 1: If an opponent plays "Turncoat", the beggar changes his faction!
Note 2: If that squire is removed from the game after a War event or he's killed (the new Assassin card), the card is removed too.

The Pizza Theory: Anchovy Expansion, consisting of a set of 16 anchovy toppings and a rulebook, is used with the Pizza Theory board game to present players with an additional challenge.

In addition to removing the opponents' toppings, each player must now work to keep the anchovies from taking over the pie, too. If all 16 anchovies end up on the pie before any player places his toppings, then everyone loses and the anchovies reign supreme!

GolFUNator is a strategic golf board game.Each player begins with the ability of an average Pro Golfer (stats taken from the 2010 PGA Tour Season). The course, Highland Peaks is a ficticional par 72 course.

Players choose the type of shot they will take. Choices range from safe, normal, or all-out from the tee, and safe, middle (of the green) or Go (for the pin) on approach shots. There are also other choices during play. On putts from the fringe and distances over 25', the player may choose 'GO' or 'LAG'. Almost anything that can happen in real golf, can happen in GolFUNator. 6 times per round, if the player hits an errant shot (trouble) he may choose to use one of his/her 6 'LUCK' cards. The results will be either good, bad, or no luck at all. 
A player can get HOT by getting 2 birdies in a row. He/she may also become COLD with 2 bogeys in a row.

The game comes with a 14x14" HD laminated cardstock 2-sided board, 2 10-sided dice, 5 decks of cards, markers, scoresheets and instructions.

The fourth supplement for Impetus. Including a campaign game based on Alexander the Great conquest, a couple of tutorials, errata and optional rules, and the following official army lists:

THE AGE OF HOPLITES
Athens (2 lists)
Thebes
Sparta
Other cities (Corinthus, Megara, Argos)

Thracians

ALEXANDER THE GREAT AND THE SUCCESSORS

Alexander macedoanians
Alexander Empire

Classical Indians

Middle Ptolemaic
Later Ptolemaic

ROME AND THE MEDITERRANEAN SEA
Middle Republican Romans
Carthaginians
Iberian and Lusitanians
Celtiberians
Gauls
Numidians
Later Numidians

Later Seleucids
family: History: Greek & Persian conflicts of the 4th century BC
History: Wars of Alexander the Great
History: Wars of the Diadochi and Syrian Wars

description:
An extraordinary game.
The game is played on any reasonable sized table. 2, 4 or 6 players can play.

This must be a forerunner of football games like Subbuteo. But, instead of little footballers that you flick, in this game you where the football boots. The diagram illustrates this rather well. You kick the ball literally with your boot-encased finger.

The rules allow for free kicks, throw-ins and penalties.

There is a warning that your boot fingers must be on the table at all times, so that should be very friendly with six players, three a side!

boardgame: 3 mit Kopf & Pfote: Fit, pfiffig, schlau
description: In Play with the coloured balls a lot of disorder has been created, the winner is the first to gather his balls back together, but to be able to do that one must answer questions, do gymnastics with the dog and solve puzzles. Each player has a collecting table and in his turn rolls the die and moves. Depending on the colour of the animal track a ball chip is turned over and one takes a card from the thus determined card stack. The rabbit poses a riddle, fun or logic question, the parrot a knowledge question and the dog sets a movement or a pantomime task. For a correct solution the player receives the chip for his table.

Players take turns rolling to move around the board where they can buy properties, gain permanent upgrades, defeat monsters and avoid traps, and duel each other for dominance.

Monsters and Traps are on High and Low Level Dungeon cards which players can choose from when they land on a Dungeon tile.  After a successful roll (and associated modifiers), you can win gold pieces.  Unsuccessful rolls reduce your hit points.

Properties can be purchased and upgraded with housing, affecting the amount of rent opponents must pay when they land on your property.  If you collect enough properties of certain types, you can gain control of the castle in that territory. The right to own properties can also be dueled over by rolling against each other using various modifiers.

To win the game, you must be the first player to complete two objectives: gain control of a castle and defeat the dragon.

The game strives to make the 'opoly style genre more fun and engaging. RPG elements, easy to learn mechanics and fast paced gameplay make it simply superior to it's predecessors. It was updated in 2023 for a smoother gameplay experience, and streamlined to be playable in 90 minutes.

The game can found as print-on-demand on The Game Crafter.
https://www.thegamecrafter.com/games/castlecontrol

It is also available for playtesting using TableTop Simulator on the Steam Workshop.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
'Flicking through the Ages' is a 2-player dexterity civilization game created as part of the NaGaDeMon contest during the month of November 2011.  Aside from the obvious Through the Ages nod, FttA claims Flicochet, Tumblin Dice, Breakout and the classic carnival game of knocking over milk bottles as its primary pedigree.

FttA aims to deliver the civilization 'feel' to a dexterity game playable in under 30 minutes and assembled from easy to find materials.  It combines five completely different minigames into a timeless struggle for cultural supremacy!  Players balance their actions by using population discs in the following ways:

1. Gather resources by flicking your disc down a platform decreasing in size with increased return for increased risk.
2. Research new technologies by flicking your disc at one of four stacks of available technologies arranged like a breakout game and taking the block you strike.  Once obtained, players need to choose whether or not to add the technology to their list of abilities or to trade in the block to increase their population
3. Declare war on the other player's civilization by flicking your disc at their researched technologies stacked in a pyramid.  Knock your opponent's blocks down to steal population, technology or colonies
4. Vie for colonies by flicking your disc closer to the colony disc than your opponents
5. Construct Wonders of the World by stacking resource cubes on a disc and sliding it along a path symbolizing the shape of the building without letting one fall

NOTES:

Construction of 'Flicking Through the Ages' requires:
1 set of Jenga-style blocks
1 set (2 colors) of 'bakelite'-style backgammon discs or other discs of roughly 1.25" diameter that flick reasonably well and are heavy enough to impact a tower of Jenga blocks enough to fall over
1 set of the rules including the 'colony' and 'technology' sticker sets to be applied (or taped) to the above components

i See it! - Is a fast-action Electronic word trivia game, where questions light up in a LED display Globe.  There are 12 categories and 6 different versions of the game.  The game includes more than 7200 challenges.  Players try to see what the answer is and hit their buzzer to stop the word as it comes up, then they have to say what it is by seeing it first!

A memorization game for kids aged 2.5 to 5, the game comes with 9 cute soft plastic animals & insects, flower tokens, and a little box. The players are shown all the animals, then while they close their eyes one animal is hidden inside the box. The players then have to take a guess which animal was hidden in the box. The one who's right gets a flower token. First player to earn five tokens wins!

Objective: be the first to get rid of all your cards

1. each player receives 5 cards (randomly) - other cards will be placed in a stack on the back onto the board
2. turn the top card and place it according the illustrated letter on the board
3a. player left to the starting may place a card with a letter, part of the previous placed card
3b. same player may place a new/additional card on the board  if the leading letter is part of previous placed card (3a)
3c. player may continue according above mentioned rule
4. if he/she can't place an additional card next player may 
act in similar way, but if no card can be played a new/additional card must be taken
5. if you empty your hand - you win
golden chip is used to mark the card last played
game was published for two different target groups
children and addults - see editions/images

Objective: be the first to arrive at your tent with all 4 parts of a golden mask 
players are acting by turns, using 2 dice
1 die has to be used for your meeple, the other to move a lion
players dig for gold - better: parts of a mask
each part buried four times - but you must find 4 matching parts
you may dug, block (with lions), steal whatever brings you closer to the goal to leave the others behind...

Objective: be the 1st to bring 3 of your 4 planes down to the airport
merely a 'Roll and move' game with one exception:
the action 'Positionswechsel' which means 'change of position'
the more players the better because if the board is 'crowded' this action can be worked out easily
==> even if your plane(s) trail behind there's a chance to win

In the summer of 1945 the United States had a firm grasp of the Pacific Theater. Japans once mighty fleet was all but shattered and unable to support any garrisoned island against an U.S. attack. With Iwo Jima taken earlier in the year the U.S. had an island to land damaged bombers that were hitting the mainland island. To launch an invasion of the main island of Japan the U.S. would need a bigger stepping stone to launch from; Okinawa was the answer.

This is a 2 player game set on the island of Okinawa and surrounding waters that recreates the battle between U.S. and Japanese forces during World War 2. The basic rules follow the concepts laid out in Axis & Allies, but combat resolution has been modified to a D12 system. The game can be played to 10 turns or 20 depending on the length of time desired; and victory is determined by the number of airbases held or carriers destroyed. The map is divided into areas that are controlled by the players' armies represented by the plastic Axis & Allies pieces.

The rules incorporate a number of new features to the Axis & Allies style of play including terrain effects, country specific units, and all new unit types including flame tanks and LCMs.

The young witches Bibi, Schubia, Flauipaui and Arkadia need to pass their exams at the end of the year.

Hextrankkunde tests dexterity. You receive one straw and 5 ingredient chips and try so suck up a chip with the straw and place it into the cauldron. The first player to place all his chips into the cauldron wins the game.

The young witches Bibi, Schubia, Flauipaui and Arkadia need to pass their exams at the end of the year.

Krötenhopsen again tests dexterity. You have one try per toad to snip it into the pond with yor finger. Each toad ending up in the pond, totally or in part, scores 1 point. If the toad touches the water lily, the score is 2 points. The player with the majority of points after 3 rounds wins the game.

The young witches Bibi, Schubia, Flauipaui and Arkadia need to pass their exams at the end of the year.

Spinnentanz is also a test on dexterity. Two players stand opposite to each other and have a spider fastened to their waistband in the back. Both try simultaneously to place their spider on the card. The first player to do so scores 1 point.  When everyone has player has played against everybody else, the player with the highest score wins.

The young witches Bibi, Schubia, Flauipaui and Arkadia need to pass their exams at the end of the year.

Hexwissen tests knowledge. The referee poses the questions and gives three answers - the first player to call out the correct answer receives the card.  The first player who collects four cards wins the game.

The Fat Penguin is an ice-breaker. In the game, players take turns to ask a question of the others. Players secretly choose their answer (red/no or green/yes). Then the asking player has to guess individually what the other players answered for themselves. If the player is correct, then its penguin advances.

The questions varies from everyday-life tendencies and preferences, to life/death and ethical dilemmas, and funny/dirty questions. E.g.: "Have you in the past year used someone elses toothbrush?", "Would you rather give up your mom's love (green) og the respect of your friends (red)?", "What is more unattractive: Too self-confident (green) or too shy (red)?", "Routined sex three times a week (green) or exciting sex once every third week (red)?"

The game is intended as an ice-breaker at parties. The drinking-game variant makes the players take a shot of booze/beer if they are right or wrong.

The game is published in German "Der Fette Pinguin", in English "The Fat Penguin" and in Danish "Den Fede Pingvin". There is also a smartphone App version of the game (available in Danish for iPhone, and soon in English and German and also for Android).

There are 16 characters from the stories of the little king Macius in this game. Each character consists of four cards (hat, head, belly and legs). The players roll the dice and try to collect all cards and whoever has the most complete figures at the end of the game wins.

boardgame: Die Koala Brüder: Tempo, Tempo, Freunde!
description: Frank and Buster give a party so big, that no one can really keep an eye on all the guests. Who can find the combinations of guests first? One player rolls the dice and all players simultaneously look for the characters rolled. They can be on one or more cards, but must be adjacent do each other, diagonal combinations do not count. For a correct combination a player receives all tiles in the combination, for an error one looses a card. When the draw pile is finished the player with the most cards wins.

boardgame: Ancient Battles Deluxe Expansion Kit 5.1: Way of the Samurai
description:
Experience the Sengoku Jidai battles of two opposing Eastern and Western forces, with 15 glorious scenarios, in Way of the Samurai.

Notice: This Expansion Kit is not a ‘stand alone’ game. To use it, you must own a copy of:

• Ancient Battles Deluxe
• Ancient Battles Deluxe Expansion Kit 5: Design Your Own

Game Data:

Complexity: 4 on a 9 scale
Solitaire Suitability: 6 on a 9 scale
Time scale: Each Game Turn equals 45 minutes
Unit scale: Dozens to hundreds of men, beasts and equipment

Game Components:

• 80 color, 5/8" square counters
• 1 Rules sheet
• 15 Original, glorious custom-designed scenarios

         Okehazama, 1560 AD
         4th Kawanakajima, 1561 AD
         The Anagawa, 1570 AD
         Mikata-Ga-Hara, 1573 AD
         Nagashino, 1575 AD
         Tedorigawa, 1577 AD
         Yamazaki, 1582 AD
         Shizugatake, 1583 AD
         Komaki, 1584 AD
         Nagakute, 1584 AD
         Hitotoribashi, 1585 AD
         Hetsugigawa, 1586 AD
         Surigehara, 1589 AD
         Sekigahara, 1600 AD
         Tennoji, 1615 AD

A mini-expansion consisting of 10 cards that introduces "Badges" to the game.

A Badge can be drawn at random by a Unit that obtains a "Battle Cry" RWB. The Badges have a permanent effect for the Unit (much like a Specialist), usually helping its Unit obtain RWB or Awards more easily.

"Badges" also comes with a Badge-related Award (the "Efficiency Medal") and with rules (printed on 2 rule cards).

This expansion was given as a thank you gift to all the supporters of D-Day Dice's 2011 Kickstarter campaign, and will become available later in 2012 as an exclusive on Valley Games' website.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Organizations: The Game Artisans of Canada

description: Invented by a Korean dad who wanted to help his kids learn how to master and conquer math. Throughout the game, you simply add up 3 tiles to make a sum of "10," 3 and 4 tiles to make a sum of "20." Each color is played separately and the black tiles are joker tiles. The goal of the game is to get rid of your tiles first!

Minions brings the classic web-based style game called 'Tower Defense' to your table top. Two criminal masterminds send waves of minions to destroy the enemy HQ. To keep the minions away, each player deploys a variety of towers to eliminate those pesky minions. The first player to sneak three minions through their enemy's defensive perimeter succeeds in destroying the enemy HQ and winning the game.

Minions uses a variable map system allowing different paths each time you play. Machine Guns, Cannons, Missiles, and Death Rays abound to frustrate the Minions' attempts at destruction.

Power Trip is a thrilling, fast-paced card game that can change directions at any time. Players race to get rid of all their cards first by attempting to match or beat the cards played by others, or by playing a Clear or Power Trip card to clear the discard pile.  The first player to do so becomes the Ruler.  Be the first player to become Ruler three times to WIN THE GAME. Play out of turn to complete four of a kind and use Action and Rule cards to squash your competition.

Easily score your Power Trip games and keep players on the edge of their seats by generating new and existing rule cards with the iPhone and Android Power Trip app.

An abstract strategy game for 2 players that adds a twist to the classic black vs. white setup: grey pieces that belong equally to both players.

The game mechanism revolves around enclosure (either partial or full) of other, single pieces on a square grid board.  Surrounded pieces can be either removed from play, replaced with upgraded pieces, or can cause extra turns or even a win, depending on the degree of enclosure (i.e. being surrounded by either 6, 7, or 8 pieces belonging to the player) and also the color of central piece being enclosed.

A turn consists of a player either placing or moving a piece, and then acting out consequences if their move results in one or more enclosure situations.  In standard game play, the color of the piece to be played (either the player's own color or grey) is decided by the flip of a fair coin, but the game can also be played without that element of chance by simply alternating between the two.

The game ends either when a player causes a piece to be entirely enclosed with pieces belonging to them filling all 8 surrounding squares (in which case they are the winner), or in the event that no open spaces remain on the board (in which case the player with the most pieces of their specific color is the winner.)

The game is both fun for beginners and yet dynamic enough to also engage experienced strategists.

Viva la ResistANTS! is an abstract boardgame. Main game mechanics includes area controll, set collection and 'press your luck'. It combines American and European styles of boardgaming design. The game has a unique setting. The forces of Resistance of Ants led by the mysterious Serg Ant try to help the civilians survive under the occupation of enemy forces, who perfidiously attacked the peaceful republic of ants. Now you must fight for your freedom. Let's say together: Viva la ResistANTS!

Each player has a detachment of 10 ants. Each turn the player can send 2 of them to the field to participate in a special mission or withdraw 1-3 of them. At the start of the game the playfield is generated randomly. If 1 or more ants get status missing in action, players should shuffle the tiles of the playfield and generate it randomly again. Other ants return to players. The ants get status missing in action if a card from alert deck with the appropriate colour is revealed for the second time. One card from alert deck is revealed every time each player takes a turn. If the same colour is revealed for the third time the alert deck must be shuffled. If it must be shuffled for the second time, the game is over. The player who gets more victory points becomes a winner. In case of a tie, the winner is the player who retained more ants.

The players have to collect 8 knowledge chips of every color. The chips are rewarded for answering one of 2200 questions out of different areas
During player's turn die is rolled; he/she may move into any direction, but not vice versa; occupied spaces may be crossed.
Appropriate card is drawn, die is used to pick a question - if the answer is correct, player may add an additional turn - moving again. You pick a chip if you find a correct answer for a space on the outer circle.

A fast and fun party game based on questions and dexterity!

A good materials inside the box. You must Slam the plastic hammers on a category to answer at the questions!
During each round the reader in turn must take an Action card and follow what it says: for example, you must read the question and talk with a "french accent" or cover an eye with your hand.
Moreover, the player reads the question without reading the category; who knows the answer must slam the hammer on a board in the space of the correct category!

A fast party game from 3 to 24 players!

Based on a popoular TV Show aired in Italy from 12 years continuously; the "Iene" are journalists who report on investigations of fraud and political problems.

In the Game "Le IENE" each player has a secret rule: he can be a "Iena" or a Cheater.

The Cheaters characters are: the doctor, the medium, the manufacturer and the politician.

In his turn the player can:
- choose an area in the city and gain money for all the cheaters pawns in that area.
- move a character pawns to one area from another and take objects.
- accuse another player to be a Cheater.
- interview another player, so he can have more information about his identity.

There will not possible to gain money or take objects when a "Iena" pawns is in that area. Infact the cheaters can make illagal traffic of money when the "Iene" aren't there for an interview!

At the end, if the "Iene" players found the right association between players-cheaters they win: otherwise, players gain/loose money according to their accusations, objects and interviews and the player with most money will be the winner.

Based on a TV Quiz Show aired in Italy.

There are more than 300 different cards with a transformed or masked image that represents a famous person, an animal, an object, a monument, a painting or a sculpture.

The active player must to recognize the subject of the pic and gain points.

Fun little game, with 4 different variants, based on good observation and quick thinking.

Objective: collect as many quality coconuts as possible
- moving by turns -
1. Preparation of the game - place all coconut tiles face down (good and bad) on dedicated fields
2. roll the die and move - if the die shows the tiger move back to your starting place
3. if you step on a coconut - pick it up and check 
4. if you land on an opponent's meeple - steal a coconut and put her/him in the middle of the board (palm tree)
5. if there are only 4 or less coconuts on the board use a rolled '4' to move AND steal a coconut from a player

Die Spielidee: 2 - 4 Affen rasen auf die Lichtung und jagen nach Kokosnüssen. Dabei passiert eine ganze Menge. Der Tiger ist auch unterwegs und den mögen die Affen nicht. Wer zum Schluss die meisten Kokosnüsse (geklaut) hat, ist nicht unbedingt Sieger, denn es gibt auch faule Nüsse.

Objective: be the first to reach the finish line
- moving by turns -
1. place the meeples on the starting position
2. turn the spinner and draw a question card
3. there is one minute left to find an appropriate answer - starting with the letter the spinner is pointing on
4. if you fail roll the die - if the number matches with correspondent letter number you may move, too
5. if spinner points at a symbol act as described

You are a solitary hero fighting to survive in a world once afflicted by ancient wars between Orcs, Elves, Humans and Angels and other Races (included in the expansions Cripta, Covo, Alchimista).

It does not matter if you are a Mage, a Paladin or a Warrior (or another Class contained in Cripta, Covo, Alchimista expansions); all you have to do is to get the most powerful equipment you can find, such as handcrafted swords and shields, spells or what remains of the fire guns and hi-tech weapons produced by a now-gone civilisation.

Defend yourself and defeat your opponents. Are you ready to fight?

Warage is the ultimate turn-based strategic card game: role play, strategy and deck building merged all together for endless adventures.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description: A mathematical game for 2 to 4 players. Each player has 9 balls in his game colour. The playing field is 6x6 and numbered from 1 to 36. If it is your turn, you have one minute (egg timer) to roll 3 regular dice and arrange them in a formula with the four basic mathematical operations. The result is where you put your ball. The target space must be unoccupied. If you can not create a formula in one minute for a free space your turn ends. If you achieve 3 or more own balls in a row you get VP. If you get five own balls in a row the game ends.  The first to have no more balls ends the game also. Player with the most points wins.

Following up on their successful Kickstarter campaign to get their game, Cards Against Humanity, professionally published, the creators are back with a card expansion that adds more hilarity for us horrible people that like to play.

In this card game, players take turns being the Card Czar, which means they will read a black question card or a fill-in-the-blank statement card.  The other players will select a white answer card from the 10 in their hand that they think best or most humorously fits the question/statement.  The Card Czar will then shuffle the answers and choose the one they like best, with that answer's owner receiving the black card as his "awesome point" for winning the round.

Once a player has at least one awesome point, they can wager an awesome point when giving a white answer card in order to throw in a second answer card, giving that player a better chance to be chosen.  The winner of the round will receive the original black card as well as any that are wagered in that round.

Play continues until a pre-determined score is reached, or until everyone is tired from laughing so hard.

This expansion adds 20 black question/statement cards and 80 white answer cards to the deck as well as 12 blank cards for players to come up with their own card ideas.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Objective: collect as many precious treasurers as possible & try to block other players
- moving by turns -
1. shuffle treasure cards and put 3 of them upside down on the board - place all the track tiles,too
2. roll the die - move your meeple into direction of a treasure
3. at the end of your turn you may turn a track tile at 90° degree to the left or right (blocking or preparing your route)
4. if you roll a 'wand' you may exchange one of your treasures for another player's one (different values)
5. if all treasures a collected count up the values printed on the tile

In Shake Shake Tower – this game's original name – players start the game with eight 3D curved polyomino pieces in a single color, then take turns placing them on a swiveling, seesaw-style platform. Gravity pulls the platform and pieces from side to side as the tower rises, so players might have a tough time adding a piece to the stack – especially since each level must be filled before players can play on top of other bricks. If a player can't legally play, she must skip her turn, so play strategically to block others while leaving yourself room to grow!

If you knock pieces off the tower, you keep them on the side, losing one point for each brick in such a piece. When a player plays her final piece, the game ends. Players then score one point for each brick of theirs visible from a top-down view of the tower. Whoever has the high score wins.

Tremblex, a revised version of Shake Shake Tower, uses the same pieces and nearly the same scoring method, but players are free to place pieces as they wish on the tower – with one small exception that drives play in a different direction. Each piece you place must touch one of your pieces already in play; if all of your pieces have been covered by other players, then you're forced to sit out until someone else knocks pieces off the tower! Once this happens, all players are back in the game. The game ends when a player places her final piece, and players tally their scores, earning one point for each brick showing from a top-down view of the tower, losing one point for each brick in pieces knocked off the tower, and collecting a three point bonus if their piece is highest up in the tower.

Tremblex includes variant rules as well, such as a cooperative mode in which players try to complete the tower with no holes and a two-player game in which opponents place pieces only on their half of the platform, trying to scale higher than the other player in order to win.

Fury, a game of fantasy warfare, brings the Manoeuvre game system to the lands of the West. Knights and Orcs fight against Dragons and Dwarves to decide the outcome of the 2nd War of the West. Fury takes the bones of Manoeuvre and builds a game that has more of everything you like in the original. More and varied types of movement such as flight and teleportation, more combats in a turn each with their own deadly styles, and devastating spells that can turn the tides of war in your favor. Your ability to control the Fury of the battlefield will determine your fate.

Fury includes armies for each of the nations of the west including; the Elves of the Woodland Realm, The Dwarves of the Kingdom of Zikkim, The Theocracy of Tayanna, The Nik’tmarg a loose conglomeration of humanoids and savage men, Badari the desert empire, The Republic of Toren, Medis the feudal lands of the wizards and Tireisis feared by all as the domain of the Necromancer. In addition Fury adds in many small nations and mercenary bands that can swing a battle in your favor.

The map boards now include mountains and desert in addition to the basic land types introduced in Manoeuvre. Other additions are territorial objectives, spells, various methods of Attack and Defense. The game includes more and varied types of Movement, expanded leader capabilities and involvement. The card mix also allows more combat from every unit through the use of generic nationality cards that can be utilized by any unit on your side. Fury turns the orderly Napoleonic battlefield on its head and populates it with Goblins, Zealots and Zombies ready to fight for their cause.

For many more details on each nation as well as the mercenary units, visit Jeff Horger's BGG design blog.

description:

CLIQUE : The Card Game

Welcome to CLIQUE, the game of ruthless popularity. You play as that most terrifying of creatures, the pretty, popular, teenage girl. The goal is simple, to accumulate enough popularity to take over the Clique and rule the school. You accomplish this by gaining the things all girls need for societal domination, The Car, The Look, The Gear and the ultimate accessory: The Guy. But your opponents, the other girls in the Clique are out to stop you with that most powerful and destructive of weapons: Gossip. Is that Look So Last Season? Is your new Car actually Used? Is your new Guy Secretly Gay? Get all four Acquisitions, free yourself from the vicious Gossip, and you can take over the Clique.

Mechanics

The deck breaks down like this:

6 Avatars - These are the girls, each with a unique look and style.
6 Looks - From Goth to Ghetto Fabulous.
6 Cars - Will you go for Classic or Import?
6 Guys - If Jock is not to your liking, how about Vampire Chic?
6 Gears - Do I go for the Jewelry or take the Dog?
24 Gossip - Hand Me Down? Late Bloomer? Cruelty in card form.
30 Specials - Remove Gossip or dramatically alter the game.
50 Status - 10, 20, or 50 point cards to measure your popularity.
And so we come to gameplay. I'll try to keep this simple. The players set what level of popularity they need to reach in order to win. Each turn you play from your hand to try to get Acquisitions (The Gear, The Guy, etc.) for yourself. Each one gives you 10 points each round toward that goal. But the other players can put Gossip on them. While an Acquisition has a Gossip card on it, it earns you nothing. Of course, turnabout is fair play, so you'll be attacking them with the same vicious rumors. The first to earn enough popularity while having all four Acquisitions in play with no Gossip on them takes over the Clique and rules the school.

Along the way, there are also unique cards to, for instance, prevent Gossip being played on your Car (My Daddy Owns A Dealership). It possible that everyone might lose their Guys in one fell swoop if someone should play Smoking In The Boys Room. The game can change dramatically from turn to turn. But isn't that the way with high school popularity? One day you are the queen, the next you are the outcast. Same goes for CLIQUE.

There Must Be No Competition, Before World Domination

In Hench: The Game, you take on the role of an evil mad scientist that has the means to rule the world, but so do a few others just like you! Before you can take over the world, you need to eliminate the competition.

Hench: The Game will be a simple card and dice game (recommended for 2-4 players). From your hand of 6 cards, you build your army of henchmen to protect you using Recruit Cards, and who will also do all your dirty work for you. You can strengthen your henchmen army by building enhancements to your secret hideout or equipping them with weapons. You can also attack, sabotage, defend yourself and many other things using Event Cards from your hand.

Basic gameplay will go like this:

1.As your turn begins, you may play a Recruit Card to play henchmen from your hand (but keeping some hidden in your hand may be beneficial to you later)
2.You then can play one Enhancement, Weapon or Event card from your hand (see FAQ for card type explanation)
3.If an Event card is played, instead of a Weapon or Enhancement card, then all effects from the card are resolved
4.Draw back up to 6 cards.
5.Next player's turn

If an Event card, such as Direct Attack or Provoked Attack, are played, then combat starts. How combat works is your opponent and yourself add up your "Hench Power", which is +1 for each henchman you have in play, and any modifiers from Weapon or Enhancements in play. Each battling player then rolls one D6 and adds it to their "Hench Power". Higher number wins, and the loser can either lose a location enhancement or half the difference between the "Hench Power" numbers (round up) in henchmen they have in play.

Some other Event cards would allow you to avoid a battle (but you end up losing a turn), forcing a battle between two other players, performing suicide attacks, etc.You lose when all of your henchmen are destroyed, and the last player standing wins the game, and the world!

The players want to cook marmalade with the mouse and collect the fruits in the garden to exchange them for marmalade. Each player draws a secret mouse card, his aim is to reach a corner square with the mouse of that colour and as many fruits as possible. The active player rolls the die and moves any mouse accordingly in any direction, jumping over other mice is possible, only one mouse per square is allowed. Who reaches a corner, discards the mouse card and draws a new one, the fruit chips he keeps. Three fruit chips are exchanged for a marmalade jar. When all the mouse cards have been discarded, the player with the most marmalade jars wins.

Four simple games teach how to remember stories and facts and how to retell them properly. This trains fantasy and verbal development. 
"Unterwegs mit der Maus" is a combination game, cards must be put on the boards, the situations have a time sequence. 
"Schnell geschaut" is a reaction game, a game master combines 3 fitting and one non-fitting card, players score a point for finding the misfit. 
"Was könnte das sein" is a description game, players must name the words describes. 
"Quatschgeschichten" is a repetition game, the first player tells a story containing the three words on his card, the next players repeat the story and add their own things, a mistake scores a minus.

The mouse is clever and the elephant is strong and they play tug-of-war, but they need both muscles and brain to pull each other over the finish line.

The terrain cards are laid out, the tug-of-war card is placed under the middle card, flag just beneath the starting post. One player has the mouse cards, the other the elephant cards. Duck cards win against 8, 9, and 10 and lose against all others. Each player chooses a card and places it face down and are revealed at the same time. The higher card wins and the player may move the tug-of-card along one terrain card. Whoever can move the tug-of-war card over the finish line wins.

description: The jetty is rather narrow and the bear and his friends jostle each other while trying to catch a fish. boardgame: Commands & Colors: Napoleonics Expansion #3 – The Austrian Army
description:
The Austrian Army is a Coalition expansion for Commands & Colors Napoleonics.

Prior to the Napoleon wars, the Austrian Empire stretched from Italy to the Netherlands and from Poland to the Balkans. Its Position in the center of Europe, however, made it the perfect target for revolutionary France and in April 1792, France declared war on Austria. The first war lasted for 5 years. Austria renewed the war against France in 1799 and again in 1805, but in both cases was swiftly defeated. In April 1809, judging that Napoleon’s army
was bogged down with the fighting in Spain, Austria invaded Bavaria, but Napoleon recovered quickly and Austria’s defeat at Wagram led to another humiliating peace treaty.

Even though the Austrian army was the third largest in the world, the Austrians lost many more battles than they won. At the time of the Napoleonic Wars, the Austrian army was a large multi-national army, made up of Austrians, Swedes, Hungarians, Englishmen, Scots, Irishmen, Walloons, Poles, Russians, Ukrainians, Germans, Croats, Serbs, and even French royalists.
The infantry regiments were large and somewhat reliable, but the tactical formations and drill were still entrenched in the past. The cavalry, although well mounted, still applied the practice of scattering their formations in small bodies, which greatly reduced their combat effectiveness. In general, the Austrian army did enjoy many successes on a regimental level, but at the brigade, divisional and corps levels its commanders were often
woefully inadequate.

In this expansion you will find 20 historical scenarios that focus on the Austrian Army battles from 1805 to 1809 against Napoleon, plus all the new units you’ll need to field for these engagements.

boardgame: Commands & Colors: Napoleonics Expansion #4 – The Prussian Army
description:
The Prussian Army is a Coalition expansion for Commands & Colors Napoleonics.

During the Seven Years’ War, small Prussia under Frederick the Great defeated the armies of Austria, Russia, and France to increase the size of its territories two fold and become a major power in Europe. But in spite of Prussia’s reputation as a formidable fighting nation, Prussia withdrew from the First Coalition as an active opponent of the French Revolution and remain on the sidelines until the Fourth Coalition in 1806.

In 1806, the Prussian Infantry were products of a system that had not altered since the Seven Years' War. The cavalry although probably the best mounted in Europe thanks to the East Prussian horse studs, were scattered amongst the army divisions at Jena, and were committed in numerous uncoordinated attacks. In general, the lack of coordination of the arms in combat was one of the major causes of the Prussian defeat at Jena.

In the words of Napoleon…

"At Jena, the Prussian army performed the finest and most spectacular maneuvers, but I soon put a stop to this tomfoolery and taught them that to fight and to execute dazzling maneuvers and wear splendid uniforms were very different matters."

- Napoleon

In 1806 Napoleon crushed the fearsome Prussian military machine. The humiliation of military defeat and being forced to cede much of its territories forced the Prussian king, Frederick William III, into much-needed military reform. The new Prussian army that emerged became one of the finest in Europe., due in part to the organizational genius of Scharnhorst and Gneisenau and in part due to Field-Marshal Prince Blücher, who drove his regiments to fight with relentless vigor. In 1813 Prussia rejoined the war against Napoleonic France and the Prussian army under Blücher, although sometimes playing second fiddle to Austria, Russia and Great Britain, was a major force in bringing an end to Napoleon Bonaparte's rule.

In 1815 Napoleon returned to meet the English and Prussians, which would lead to his Waterloo. Prussia’s reward in 1815 at the Congress of Vienna was the recovery of her lost territories, as well as the whole of the Rhineland and Westphalia and other territories. Prussia therefore emerged from the Napoleonic Wars as the dominant power in Germany.

In this expansion you will find 18 historical scenarios that focus on the Prussian Army battles from 1806 to 1815 against Napoleon, plus all the new units you’ll need to field for these engagements.

- Richard Borg

boardgame: Commands & Colors: Napoleonics Expansion #2 – The Russian Army
description:
The Russian Army is a Coalition expansion for Commands & Colors Napoleonics.

Russia, was ruled until 1796 by Catherine the Great. It would then suffer under "Mad" Tsar Paul I, until his assassination in 1801, which brought his son Alexander I to the throne. Alexander, in command of a Russian army that was as vast as the territory from which it was drawn, did his best by various treaties and alliances to counter the increasing power of France under Napoleon without going to war.

The Russian army at the time of Napoleonic wars still had many characteristics of Peter the Great’s regime; senior officers were largely recruited from aristocratic circles, and the Russian soldier was regularly beaten and punished to instill discipline. Furthermore, many lower-level officers were poorly trained. Yet the Russians involved in hostilities with its neighbors - Sweden, Poland, Turkey and Austria - were capable of astonishing feats and total, blind obedience to orders.

In 1805 Britain and Russia signed an alliance against France. In 1806, Prussia joined the Coalition and Prussia and Russia mobilized for a fresh campaign. After Napoleon’s humiliation of Prussia at Jena, the French Emperor turned his attention to subduing his Russian foe and marched into Poland. After a series of sanguinary battles, the French drove Russian forces out of Poland back to Mother Russia and created a new Duchy of Warsaw, but made a solid treaty between the two nations at Tilsit.

In 1812, the Russo-French treaty gradually became strained, as the requirement of joining France's Continental Blockade against Great Britain was a serious disruption of Russian commerce. Bonaparte decided to bring the Russians back into line in June, and invaded Russia hoping to inflict a major defeat on the Russians and force Alexander to sue for peace. The invasion of Russia and the retreat of the French army, as many historians point out, proved to be the turning point in the Napoleonic Wars.

In 1813 Russia opened the campaign against Napoleon joined by Prussia and Austria, and during the three-day battle of Leipzig, Bonaparte's fate was sealed.

In this expansion you will find 18 historical scenarios that focus on the Russian Army battles from 1806 to 1814 against Napoleon, plus all the new units you’ll need to field for these engagements.

- Richard Borg

description:
Creative Kitchen is board game that brings together your creative and culinary skills while mixing in a few good laughs. Originally designed to help engage students at a local culinary training program in Savannah, Georgia (Starfish Café), the game has earned rave reviews from both home cooks and professional chefs in prototyping sessions.

The game is played by dealing each “chef contestant” a set of ingredient cards. Based off the mystery box ingredient concept popular in shows such as Food Network’s Chopped and Iron Chef, players can discard and pick up new ingredients in their quest to make the best recipe out of their hand. Contestants then have to name and illustrate their dish on provided recipe cards and sell it to their panel of judges (their fellow contestants!). Creative Kitchen is a great way to gather recipes from friends and share a few laughs over “interesting” recipes. Cheers to Creative Kitchen’s next master chef!

Currently, the board game design includes 110 ingredient cards (expansion decks to come!), game board, kitchen timing device, pad of blank recipe cards and eye catching Creative Kitchen box.

Altercation - The Card Slinging Action Game

Object of the game:
Players win the game by either Knocking Out all other players 
OR
By being the player with a Vigor Point Total(VPT) Closest to the Target Number (TN) (in either direction) when all cards have been played from the deck and all players hands. *If two players are tied for Closest to TN, The Throwaway Pile gets shuffled and play continues*

How to Play: 
1. Calculate Vigor Point Total (VPT): Roll the 4 dice add the total together: That is each players starting VPT. Write each players name and VPT on the score sheet. 
2. Calculate Target Number (TN): Roll 3 dice and add the total. This is the number everyone is trying to get closest to by the end of the game. 
3. Decide who plays first. Roll a dice, Flip a coin, etc.  
4. Shuffle the Deck and Deal each Player 5 cards. 
5. Place the deck face down in the middle of the table. 
6. The first player takes their Turn and play continues to the next player on the left.

Turn:
Step 1: Draw cards until you have 5 in you hand (skip this step on the first turn) 
Step 2: Do 1 of the following 2 options: 
A. Play a card. Choose a player and play a Lose, Gain, (put this 
card in the Throw Away pile next to the deck after addition or 
subtraction takes place) or Defender Card (play this card in 
front of you) 
B. Throw Away  1 Card and Draw 1 new Card 
Step 3: End of Turn

In this game, you are managing a fictional National Park in the U.S.  your goal is to build improvements into the park that attract more visitors.  Unfortunately, more visitors bring more problems and nature is always trying to reclaim the land.  Every round you will build you park and accrue visitor points, while one of your opponent's will take nature's revenge and another opponent will play the unruly visitors who will damage the park.  At the end of every year, a visitor assessment is performed for each of the improvement categories and the number of visitor points you have accumulated in each category  that will come to your park the following year are assigned.  This is also the number of improvement points that you will receive that year to improve your park.

At the end of the last round, you tally up all of the visitor points you have accrued and receive bonuses for having the majority in each category.  Highest score wins.

GAME ON is the very first live card game.
The Game is played while watching a sports event.

There is a special deck made for each sport:

     Baseball
     Football
     Hockey
     Basketball
     Soccer
     Golf
     Tennis


The two most popular ways of playing GAME ON….

Each player (2-5 players) are dealt 7 random cards. Leave the leftover cards in a pile, they will be used soon.  Now.....

1. Let the sports event decide what cards to play by what is actually happening in the game. The goal is to dump all of your cards first. If the next play is running back running for 15+ yards and you have that card then you can play it. That is once less card you have to dump AND if that card you use is a + or - card then you get to dump more of your cards or have a player (or two) pick more cards up from the leftover pile. Its that simple.....AND FUN!!

2. Play your cards by predicting what will happen next in the game. The goal is to collect as many cards as possible. Before the next chance (inning, possession, break, etc...) each player chooses a play from the hand they are dealt. Place it face up so everyone knows whos playing what. When the first person who chose correctly wins then he/she gets to collect all the cards that were played. Plus if his/her card was a +/- card then she could get more cards from the leftover pile or they can choose the cards that other players have to dump. Trade cards are used as well and when you win with that card you get to pick a players card (blindly) to trade with your worst card. Its that simple....AND FUN!

***There are many other ways of playing GAME ON. Other luck games or harder games involving more skills and knowledge.***

Each sport has between 8-13 different plays in the deck. Some are rare but most are common plays.

Oma, Tante, Anverwandte ("Granny, Aunt, Relatives") challenges players to remember the faces of multiple oh-so-similar relatives and reproduce them.

In a round, each player receives a portrait card, looks at it secretly for some predetermined amount of time (say 15-30 seconds), lays the card down, then tries to reproduce the picture as accurately as possible. Players score points for each element that is the right shape, and more points if elements are the proper colors.

Some portraits have more elements to remember than others, so while your scoring potential is higher, you'll have a tougher time keeping everything in mind. After a certain number of rounds, the player with the most points wins.

description:
Xodd is a drawless connection and territory game for two players: Black and White. It is played on the intersections (points) of an initially empty square grid (board). The suggested board size is between 9×9 and 14×14. Both players must have access to a sufficient supply of black and white stones.

Black plays first, then turns alternate. On your turn, pass or place one or two stones of any colors on empty points. On their first turn, Black can only place one stone. At the end of your turn, there must be an odd number of groups on the board. A group is an entire set of orthogonally interconnected stones of the same color. A single stone, not connected to others of its color, is also a group.

When both players pass in succession, the game ends, and the player with fewer groups on the board wins.

				
				
					NotesDouble placement serves as both a first-move equalizer and a way to make the game playable under the group restriction rule.

Apart from the intended goal of eliminating draws (they would be common otherwise), forcing an odd number of groups at the end of a turn has some tactical implications that lend the game a loose single-placement feel. For example, groups separated by two empty points cannot be merged on a single turn, as the player would need another placement to restore the group count to an odd number. Also, the diagonal connection is safe if the opponent has exactly one or three groups touching the two empty points adjacent to both stones, and unsafe otherwise.

Endgames are also quite tricky under this rule: you will often want to merge two of your own groups or create another enemy group, but you will be unable to do so because you can no longer restore the group count to an odd number.

Guerilla ! is a miniature skirmish ruleset published in Vae Victis magazine issue #15. The game simulates battles of the mid-nineteenth century, especially the many colonial wars of the era.

The game uses a simple chit-pull system to alternate figure activation. An activated figure can move and/or fire, reload a weapon or attack in close combat. The rules include the different firearms available at the time (muzzle- and breach-loading rifles, pistols and carbines), cavalry, cover, morale, experience levels (a veteran figure will reload a weapon faster and have bonuses to morale, shooting and close combat), and a scenario simulating the last moments of the battle of Camerone.

Scale: 1 mini = 1 man, 1 cm = 1 m, a turn = a few seconds

An Offal Game for 1 or 2 players

Pity the poor people of Cadaver City. Long ago they filled up all their cemeteries with their loved (and not so loved) ones. Now they’ve run out of room so they have taken to dumping the bodies down the sewer (out of sight out of mind). You, enterprising 3rd Class Sewer Engineers (Plungers), see a chance to make some fast money and solve the corpse clog threatening to overwhelm the existing system. At a quiet back door behind the Treatment Plant a line of customers queue up for the bits and pieces you have on offer. Deliver them the right parts, aged to the right amount of time and you’ll receive your reward.

Needed to play: The deck of 27 cards
10 eurocubes in each of 4 colors for the body parts (white for Bones, brown for Organs, gray for Brains and red for Hearts) and 9 black eurocubes for the rats
1 eurocube marker in each of 2 other colors to keep track of player scores on the Gore Track
1 large bag [or an old sock] (the Bag O’ Parts) to hold all the body parts, 10 small ziplock bags to act as body bags in the game and 1 four-sided die

Setup: Place the 4 Sewer Pipe cards,  Gore Track, and the score markers between the Plungers (players).
Shuffle the customer deck and deal 2 customers to each Plunger, placing the rest in a face down draw pile known as “The Queue”, next to the Sewer Pipe. Each plunger should place their customers face up in front of them.
Shuffle the tool deck and deal 3 tools to each Plunger that they will use as their initial hand of cards. The remaining tool cards will be placed in a tool card draw deck next to The Queue.
Place the body bags within reach of both Plungers. Each Plunger should take 1 body bag for each customer they have during the game. Lastly place all the body parts and rats in the Bag O’ Parts.

Phase 1: Adding new body parts
Roll 1d4 and add that value to the number of waiting customers in front of them. The Plunger will then blind draw that number of body parts from the Bag O’ Parts. Shift all existing body parts towards The Clog to make room for the incoming body parts. Rats will never move. Set aside any body parts that are shifted off of the Day 3 card into a reserve pile. Place the new body parts in the order they are drawn on the Sewer Pipe cards beginning from the rightmost space on the Day 1 card. If any rats are drawn they will be placed on the Rat spaces beginning from the far left of the Day 3 card. Any body part occupying a Rat space where a Rat will be placed, during this phase, will be removed and tossed back into the Bag O’ Parts.

Roll 1d4 and place that many reserved Day 3 parts on The Clog card spaces, tossing the rest back in the Bag O’ Parts.

Phase 2: Filling orders
Each customer card lists the number of parts required to fill the order.

To fill an order a Plunger must choose all of any of 1 type of body parts on any one Sewer card (meeting the desired aging requirement) placing those parts in the customer’s body bag. Any extra body parts not needed for the orders go back in the Bag O’ Parts. Plungers may use the body parts to attempt to fill 1, 2 or more customer orders as long as the body parts are of the desired type and aging. If a customer’s complete order is filled the Plunger scores the money value, indicated in the lower right corner of the customer card, on the Gore Track. All body parts from this order are then tossed back in the Bag O’ Parts. A new customer is then drawn from The Queue.

Tool Cards: Each Plunger may use one of their tool cards at any point during their turn. Once played, the card will be placed on a discard pile next to the tool card draw pile and a new card is drawn.

Game End: The game ends in one of two ways:
a. The Clog card has become full of body parts. In this case, the current Plunger finishes their turn and any final scoring is marked.
b. All 10 customers have had their orders filled.

The Plunger who has earned the most money is declared the winner and receives a promotion to 2nd Class Sewer Engineer.

This game can be played solo as well. See the rules for modifications to the game.

Iron & Steam is a rules system for playing out ironclad naval combat in the era of the American Civil War. Game play is relatively streamlined, while rules include a good amount of specific technical information to satisfy those wanting a greater degree of granularity and detail.  As such, the rules contain pre-generated game statistics for historical vessels, but also provide guidelines for converting any vessel to in-game play statistics.  Similarly, for simplicity identical gun ranges are suggested for any gun of a given type (e.g. smoothbore cannon), while for those players wishing to add more detail and realism an appendix is included with expanded gun statistics to differentiate historical guns of various manufacture.

The rules are designed to use a scale of 1:2400

Also included is a bibliography of suggested reading.

A game for two that tests your powers of observation. There are two decks of cards, 40 cards per deck. The cards show a grid of 6 squares 4 of which are blank and two have either a red triangle or a red circle, or, indeed, a blue triangle or a blue circle. Each card will have a red and a blue example of the shape.

Each player shuffles their deck and then gives it to his or her opponent. Interestingly, it is allowed to shuffle them with some cards the wrong way round. Not sure if this adds to the difficulty or not.

At the word Go, both players start searching through the deck they have been given. The object is to find a matching pair. No two cards are identical so a pair is two cards that have the two shapes reversed, so to speak. For example, if a card had a Blue triangle in the top left corner and a red circle in the bottom right corner, the the matching card will be one thast has a red triangle in the top left corner and a blue triangle in the bottom right corner.

The first player to match the 20 pairs available, wins

This was an expansion available to Kickstarter Backers of Crash Games' November 2011 Kickstarter Campaign for Rise!

Rise! The Ballistic Expansion Pack adds three new types of pieces with a more "take that" element to Rise!

The Anchor Piece- The Anchor Piece is nearly an immovable object. The Anchor Piece doesn't move except when he meets another one of our special pieces which we'll get to in just a minute. The Anchor Piece can wreak quite a bit of havoc when placed near an opponent's freshly laid foundation of one of their towers.

The Vortex Piece - The Vortex Piece swallows everything in its path! When placed the Vortex Piece begins its timer and your opponent has four turns to disarm The Vortex Piece. The theme is based on the darkride "Carnaval Festival" in "De Efteling", a theme park in The Netherlands. The ride is similar to the "It's a small world" ride of Disney fame, but with more red noses.

Wizard's Tournament

Every year, wizards from all over the realm come together to fight over the title of Archmage to become the most powerful wizard in the world.  Wizard's Tournament is a fantasy deck building game of magic combat where two players attempt to defeat each other using spells, monsters, and relics.  The game comes with thirty-five different sets of cards but only twelve of these card sets are picked at random to be used during a game.  These cards are added to a player's deck during a game so every time you play, it will feel like a new and exciting game.

Spell Cards
Spell cards allow a player to perform special actions including dealing damage, drawing cards, healing, and causing an opponent to discard cards.  When cast, the ability of a Spell card occurs once and then it is discarded.  Fast spells can be played at any time but regular spells can only be cast on the owner's turn.  Spell cards can also be discarded to add Spirit to your Spirit Pool.

Monster Cards
Monsters are permanent creatures used to attack your opponent and defend you from enemy monsters.  Every monster has a special ability that makes them more dangerous and difficult to defeat.

Relic Cards
These permanent artifacts give a player special abilities that allow them to perform an action every turn until they are destroyed or stolen.

Game Play
Each player starts with thirty Life.  If a player reduced his opponent's Life to zero, he wins the game.  A player can also win by having thirty or more Spirit in his Spirit Pool or by having one hundred or more Life.

Each player starts the game with ten Spirit cards.  Twelve cards are drawn from the Selection deck and these twelve card sets are placed on the table.  The starting player is determined randomly.   Before play begins, the player that is going first draws one card and the player going second draws three cards.

There are three possible icons on the each card: Green, Blue, and Red.

Green Icons: This is the Cast cost to activate a Spell, Monster, or Relic card.  This is also the purchase price in Spirit to add this card to a player's discard pile.

Blue Icons: This is the amount of Spirit added to a player's Spirit Pool when a card with a blue icon is discarded without activating it.

Red Icons: Monster cards have red icons equal to its Power.  A monster's Power is equal to a monster's Life and the amount of damage it can deal to a player or another monster.

Turn Sequence - 5 Phases

Draw Phase

A player draws five cards from her deck.  A player may never have more than ten cards in her hand at any time.  If a player cannot draw a card because her deck has run out of cards, the player must shuffle her discard pile into a new deck.

Purchase Phase

During any phase, Spirit cards or Spell cards can be discarded to add Spirit to a player's Spirit Pool equal to the number in the blue icon on the card.  This Spirit can be spent to purchase any of the twelve cards in the center of the table.  To add a card to your discard pile, you must pay the value in the card's green icon in Spirit.  Only one copy of each card can be purchased each turn.

Magic Phase

Players can spend Spirit to cast Spell, Monster, and Relic cards.  When Spell cards are cast, their ability is performed once and then they are discarded.  When Monster and Relic cards are cast, they are placed in play with an Activation token. Relics and monsters can use their abilities during the Magic and Combat phases.  An Activation token is placed on a relic when it is activated.  Relics and monsters with an Activation token cannot be activated.

Combat Phase

A Monster card can be used to attack another player or defend you from an attacking monster.  Monsters with an Activation token on them cannot attack, defend, or use their ability.  To attack, select all the monsters under your control that you want to attack.  Your opponent then decides which of his monsters will defend against each of the attacking monsters.  More than one monster can be used to block a single attacking monster.  After the attack, place an Activation token on each of the attacking monsters.

If a monster attacks and is not blocked, the defending player’s Life is reduced by the Power of that monster.  If a monster is blocked by a defending monster, both monsters deal damage equal to their Power to his opponent.  If a monster's damage is ever equal to or greater than its Power, it is destroyed and placed in its owner’s discard pile.  If a defending monster cannot absorb all this damage, the excess damage is applied to the defending player.  Excess damage applied to an attacking monster is ignored.

If more than one monster is used to block a single attacking monster, the attacking player may apply damage equal to the Power of the attacking monster divided amongst any the defending monsters in any way.  Any of these defending monsters that receive enough damage to be destroyed are placed in his owner’s discard pile.  If a group of blocking monsters cannot absorb all the damage, the player that controls them has his Life reduced by the excess damage.  All the Power of the blocking monsters are combined and applied to the attacking monster.  If the damage dealt is equal to or greater than the Power of the attacking monster’s Power, the attacking monster is destroyed and placed in its owner’s discard pile and the excess damage is ignored.

End Phase

All Activation tokens are removed from play, all monster damage is discarded, and all Spirit Pools are emptied.

3 or 4 Player Optional Rules

The game can also be played with optional rules for three or four players when you use the extra Spirit cards included in the game.

This game was created by Jennifer Diane Reitz to make part of her fictional universe of Pastel Defender Heliotrope.

It is based on the ancient game of latrunculi or latrunculorum, where players try to eliminate all the opponent's pieces using a "flanking movement" (i.e. pieces surrounding the captured piece in opposite sides). There's little information about movement of pieces, being suggested different ways.

Latrotabuli is a variant played in 12 x 6 retangular board and players have 12 latros and 1 centurion (acts as king). Latros can move any number of spaces in one of the cardinal directions whilst the Centurion can only move one space.

One particular feature of the game is the possibility of creating several formations to best deployment of pieces: the Testudo and the Phalanx. The testudo is a 4-piece square formation and phalanx is a 4-piece linear formation, in which pieces move as one.

The method of capture depends of the capturing formation (or lack of) and of the captured piece/pieces, and can be by approach, by flanking or by moving and replacing.

There's 3 victory conditions: 

     capture all opponent latros (pieces)
     capture the opponent centurion or,
     when a player in the beginning of his turn can't move his centurion, he loses.

boardgame: White Elephant: Mystery Box Promo Cards
description:
This promo includes seven "Mystery Box" cards and was only available through Kickstarter during the project to fund the "White Elephant" card game.

Mystery Box Rules


     Deal Gift Cards to each player according to the rules in the standard game, then deal one Mystery Box card to each player. Players keep this card until their turn to be the starting player. 
     Each player simultaneously places one Gift Card from their hand face-down in the center of the table as in the standard game. The starting player (the player who will select the first gift of this party) places his or her Mystery Box card face up on top of their own Gift Card. This gift is now the "Mystery Box" gift for this round. 
     The game continues as normal with the exception that when a player selects the Mystery Box gift they do not “open” it by turning it over. The gift may be selected and stolen like any other gift without being revealed. 
     When the round is over and each player has a gift, the player with the Mystery Box gift may now look at the Gift Card without revealing it to the other players. The player, the only one who knows the color and number of that card, tucks it face-down under his or her Gift List Card. The Mystery Box card used this round may now be discarded. 
     The next round resumes with the new starting player using his or her Mystery Box card. 


Rules Updates (12/09/2011)

When played as written in the current rules, the Mystery Boxes usually didn't hold very good presents. Therefore, the designer (Brian Kelley) suggests that you follow the current rules, except for these changes:


     Before looking at their cards players randomly select one of the cards dealt to them to be their Mystery Box gift, keeping it face down.
     Players place their Mystery Box card on top of this selected card without looking at it.
     The Mystery Boxes are then kept close to each player until it is their turn to select a gift first, and then they are the ones who bring the Mystery Box to the "party."

description: The Golden Axe Expansion comes as a result of several testers saying they wished the game had two axes, it makes playing the game that much quicker, and it also gives you the opportunity for options on your turn.  Some of the variants with the Golden Axe include opting to use the Golden Axe for only ONE hit rather than the normal TWO on your turn, but if you do get anything to fall you get double the points!  This can help you catch up if you're behind... or kill you if you aren't careful.

description: This is a special colored core and bark set that can be used to add many variations to the game, it includes the golden core and four golden bark pieces.  The suggested rules are posted here on BGG.  This is GREAT for adding even more strategy to the game!  You can put all of the GOLDEN BARK in one section or spread it out over the tree as you prefer.  Each GOLDEN BARK is worth double points but beware of the Golden Core, it is twice as bad at -10 points!  You do NOT want to hit the Golden Core off!

Starzone is a print-and-play game for 2 players set in outer space. Each player battles with a fleet of spacecraft by flicking coins into the Starzone target. The Starzone target is made up of different zones, and asteroids which litter space.  Which zone you hit determines which action you take: you can repair your spacecraft, activate your shields, add or destroy asteroids on the target, or damage your opponent's spacecraft. A special Zone Shift zone on the target allows you to change which actions can be activated by hitting each zone, adding a tactical element to the game. Your spacecraft has different sectors, and the health of each sector is given by hit points. When one sector is destroyed then your spacecraft is destroyed. The first player to destroy three of their enemy's fleet wins the game.

Starzone is a fun and furious game of dexterity and strategy. Outwit your opponent using asteroids and zone-shifts. Find your enemy's weak spot and flick it into outer space!

Players take the role of MATRIX RUNNERS (2-4 players) characters with unique advantages, disadvantages & specialty RIGS which they use to hack into the Cybernet.

Players take missions, complete them, hack into systems, enter battle arenas, invest in upgrading, or purchasing programs, and taking on more & more difficult challenges, all the while dodging traces, system guardians, & other players who may be trying to collect a bounty on them.

It's always risky engaging with other players in the net.
this provides them with the ability to trade or sell each other programs, or reveal information (for a price). But you never know if the mission they're on is to catch you & destroy you.
if you get caught, you can get fined, you programs can become corrupted, or you could face a serious virus is infecting your rig, or worse, eliminated!
It is a game of covert operations, espionage, and power. The more you earn the better the hardware & software you can buy, and become better equipped to move on to bigger and better things, but you never know who could be after you & who can be your ally.

The Year is 2055
The world is run by 3 major corporations,

GLOBE-E-TRON, E-Vision, and NOVA CORPS.
Sovereignty of Countries, and Governments have become obsolete.
The once known Internet has evolved into a vast Virtual Cybernet, where millions of people live out their
fantasy in virtual entertainment. The WORLD has become solely dependent on this Cybernet.
Corporations that virtually govern the world have reputation to uphold, 
so they hire MATRIX RUNNERS to do their dirty work.
Sometimes these RUNS, include:
Hacking Important information on a new product, 
Retrieve info on corporate activity, 
Eliminate the competition by destroying their system. 
Other jobs include hunting down other hackers as well,
Because of this vast underground employment market, a secret organization has developed an agency to facilitate 
these jobs, keeping both the employer, and runner safe and anonymous.
This AGENCY also provides illegal programs to registered RUNNERS & provides them with jobs.
They are simply referred to as THE AGENCY.
It is a dangerous world of Intrigue, Deception, Skill, and Subterfuge. Noone is truly safe, and anyone can be traced and Eliminated. Tread carefully, young Runner...
